title: Brooklyn Bridge

infobox.value: 5 lanes of [roadway](/wiki/Roadway "Roadway")  [Elevated trains](/wiki/Elevated_railway "Elevated railway") (until 1944)  [Streetcars](/wiki/Streetcar "Streetcar") (until 1950)  Pedestrians and bicycles

[New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") ([Civic Center](/wiki/Civic_Center,_Manhattan "Civic Center, Manhattan"), [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") – [Dumbo](/wiki/Dumbo,_Brooklyn "Dumbo, Brooklyn")/[Brooklyn Heights](/wiki/Brooklyn_Heights "Brooklyn Heights"), [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"))
[New York City Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Transportation "New York City Department of Transportation")
127 ft (38.7 m) above mean high water[[9]](#cite_note-NOAA-10)

Free (Manhattan-bound to FDR Drive north, and Brooklyn-bound) [Variable congestion charge](/wiki/Congestion_pricing_in_New_York_City "Congestion pricing in New York City") (Manhattan-bound to all other exits)

is a [cable-stayed suspension bridge](/wiki/Cable-stayed_suspension_bridge "Cable-stayed suspension bridge") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), spanning the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") between the boroughs of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"). Opened on May 24, 1883, the Brooklyn Bridge was the first fixed crossing of the East River. It was also the [longest suspension bridge in the world](/wiki/List_of_longest_suspension_bridge_spans#History_of_longest_suspension_spans "List of longest suspension bridge spans") when opened, with a main span of 1,595.5 feet (486.3 m) and a deck 127 ft (38.7 m) above [mean high water](/wiki/Mean_high_water "Mean high water"). The span was originally called the **New York and Brooklyn Bridge** or the **East River Bridge** but was officially renamed the Brooklyn Bridge in 1915.

The **Brooklyn Bridge** is a [cable-stayed suspension bridge](/wiki/Cable-stayed_suspension_bridge "Cable-stayed suspension bridge") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), spanning the [East River](/wiki/East_River "East River") between the boroughs of [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan") and [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn"). Opened on May 24, 1883, the Brooklyn Bridge was the first fixed crossing of the East River. It was also the [longest suspension bridge in the world](/wiki/List_of_longest_suspension_bridge_spans#History_of_longest_suspension_spans "List of longest suspension bridge spans") when opened, with a main span of 1,595.5 feet (486.3 m) and a deck 127 ft (38.7 m) above [mean high water](/wiki/Mean_high_water "Mean high water"). The span was originally called the **New York and Brooklyn Bridge** or the **East River Bridge** but was officially renamed the Brooklyn Bridge in 1915.

Proposals for a bridge connecting Manhattan and Brooklyn were first made in the early 19th century; these plans evolved into what is now the Brooklyn Bridge, designed by [John A. Roebling](/wiki/John_A._Roebling "John A. Roebling"). The project's chief engineer, his son [Washington Roebling](/wiki/Washington_Roebling "Washington Roebling"), contributed further design work, assisted by the latter's wife, the engineer [Emily Warren Roebling](/wiki/Emily_Warren_Roebling "Emily Warren Roebling"). Construction started in 1870 and was overseen by the New York Bridge Company, which in turn was controlled by the [Tammany Hall](/wiki/Tammany_Hall "Tammany Hall") political machine. Numerous controversies and the novelty of the design prolonged the project over thirteen years. After opening, the Brooklyn Bridge underwent several reconfigurations, having carried horse-drawn vehicles and elevated railway lines until 1950. To alleviate increasing traffic flows, additional bridges and tunnels were built across the East River. Due to gradual deterioration, the Brooklyn Bridge was renovated several times, including in the 1950s, 1980s, and 2010s.

The Brooklyn Bridge is the southernmost of five vehicular bridges connecting Manhattan Island and [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island"), with the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge"), the [Williamsburg Bridge](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge"), the [Queensboro Bridge](/wiki/Queensboro_Bridge "Queensboro Bridge"), and the [Robert F. Kennedy Bridge](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy_Bridge "Robert F. Kennedy Bridge") (formerly known as the Triborough Bridge) to the north.[[15]](#cite_note-16) Only passenger vehicles and pedestrian and bicycle traffic are permitted. A major tourist attraction since it opened, the Brooklyn Bridge has become an icon of New York City. Over the years, the bridge has been used for stunts and performances, as well as several crimes, attacks and vandalism. The Brooklyn Bridge is designated a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"), a [New York City landmark](/wiki/New_York_City_Landmarks_Preservation_Commission "New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission"), and a [National Historic Civil Engineering Landmark](/wiki/List_of_historic_civil_engineering_landmarks "List of historic civil engineering landmarks").

## Description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Description")]

The Brooklyn Bridge, an early example of a steel-wire [suspension bridge](/wiki/Suspension_bridge "Suspension bridge"),[[16]](#cite_note-17)[[b]](#cite_note-18) uses a [hybrid](/wiki/Cable-stayed_suspension_bridge "Cable-stayed suspension bridge") design combining elements of [cable-stayed](/wiki/Cable-stayed_bridge "Cable-stayed bridge") and [suspension bridges](/wiki/Suspension_bridge "Suspension bridge"), with both vertical and diagonal suspender cables.[[17]](#cite_note-19) Its stone towers are [neo-Gothic](/wiki/Neo-gothic_architecture "Neo-gothic architecture"), with characteristic pointed arches.[[18]](#cite_note-Greene_2017_p._81-20) The [New York City Department of Transportation](/wiki/New_York_City_Department_of_Transportation "New York City Department of Transportation") (NYCDOT), which maintains the bridge, says that its original paint scheme was "Brooklyn Bridge Tan" and "Silver", but other accounts state that it was originally entirely "[Rawlins Red](/wiki/Rawlins,_Wyoming#"Rawlins_Red" "Rawlins, Wyoming")".[[19]](#cite_note-Saxena_2010_i247-21)[[20]](#cite_note-Buiso_2010_i077-22)

### Deck

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Deck")]

[![A stone viaduct approach ramp to the Brooklyn Bridge, as seen from a street in Brooklyn, with buildings to either side](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e7/DUMBO_%2896047423%29.jpg/250px-DUMBO_%2896047423%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:DUMBO_(96047423).jpg)

An approach ramp to the Brooklyn Bridge, seen from Brooklyn, with [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") (partially hidden by buildings) seen in the background

To provide sufficient clearance for shipping in the East River, the Brooklyn Bridge incorporates long approach viaducts on either end to raise it from low ground on both shores.[[7]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._29-31-7) Including approaches, the Brooklyn Bridge is a total of 6,016 feet (1,834 m) long[[2]](#cite_note-NYCL-2)[[3]](#cite_note-Facts_on_File_1914_p._839-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Citizen_Almanac_1893_p._165-4) when measured between the curbs at [Park Row](/wiki/Park_Row_(Manhattan) "Park Row (Manhattan)") in Manhattan and Sands Street in Brooklyn.[[4]](#cite_note-Brooklyn_Citizen_Almanac_1893_p._165-4) A separate measurement of 5,989 feet (1,825 m) is sometimes given; this is the distance from the curb at [Centre Street](/wiki/Centre_Street_(Manhattan) "Centre Street (Manhattan)") in Manhattan.[[5]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._2-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Barnes_p._28-6)[[7]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._29-31-7)

#### Suspension span

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Suspension span")]

The main span between the two suspension towers is 1,595.5 feet (486.3 m) long and 85 feet (26 m) wide.[[5]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._2-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Barnes_p._28-6)[[8]](#cite_note-tribune19220729-9) The bridge "elongates and contracts between the extremes of temperature from 14 to 16 inches".[[21]](#cite_note-23) Navigational clearance is 127 ft (38.7 m) above [mean high water](/wiki/Mean_high_water "Mean high water") (MHW).[[9]](#cite_note-NOAA-10)[[22]](#cite_note-nyt-2025-05-18-24) A 1909 *[Engineering Magazine](/wiki/Engineering_Magazine "Engineering Magazine")* article said that, at the center of the span, the height above MHW could fluctuate by more than 9 feet (2.7 m) due to temperature and traffic loads, while more rigid spans had a lower maximum [deflection](/wiki/Deflection_(engineering) "Deflection (engineering)").[[23]](#cite_note-25)

The side spans, between each suspension tower and each side's suspension anchorages, are 930 feet (280 m) long.[[5]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._2-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Barnes_p._28-6) At the time of construction, engineers had not yet discovered the [aerodynamics](/wiki/Aerodynamics "Aerodynamics") of bridge construction, and bridge designs were not tested in [wind tunnels](/wiki/Wind_tunnel "Wind tunnel"). John Roebling designed the Brooklyn Bridge's truss system to be six to eight times as strong as he thought it needed to be. As such, the open truss structure supporting the deck is, by its nature, subject to fewer aerodynamic problems.[[24]](#cite_note-Talbot_p._4-26)[[25]](#cite_note-27) However, due to a supplier's fraudulent substitution of inferior-quality wire in the initial construction, the bridge was reappraised at the time as being only four times as strong as necessary.[[24]](#cite_note-Talbot_p._4-26)[[26]](#cite_note-nyt19860308-28)

The main span and side spans are supported by a structure containing [trusses](/wiki/Truss "Truss") that run parallel to the roadway,[[27]](#cite_note-:1-29) each of which is 33 feet (10 m) deep.[[28]](#cite_note-Langmead_2009_p._56-30)[[29]](#cite_note-concise-description-31) Originally there were six trusses,[[27]](#cite_note-:1-29) but two were removed during a late-1940s renovation.[[30]](#cite_note-nyt19500920-32)[[31]](#cite_note-:2-33) The trusses allow the Brooklyn Bridge to hold a total load of 18,700 short tons (17,000 metric tons), a design consideration from when it originally carried heavier elevated trains.[[7]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._29-31-7)[[32]](#cite_note-34) These trusses are held up by suspender ropes, which hang downward from each of the four main cables. Crossbeams run between the trusses at the top, and diagonal and vertical stiffening beams run on the outside and inside of each roadway.[[28]](#cite_note-Langmead_2009_p._56-30)[[29]](#cite_note-concise-description-31)

An elevated pedestrian-only promenade runs in between the two roadways and 18 feet (5.5 m) above them.[[33]](#cite_note-nyt19850816-35) It typically runs 4 feet (1.2 m) below the level of the crossbeams,[[34]](#cite_note-BBPr_p._26-27-36) except at the areas surrounding each tower. Here, the promenade rises to just above the level of the crossbeams, connecting to a balcony that slightly overhangs the two roadways.[[35]](#cite_note-37) The path is generally 10 to 17 feet (3.0 to 5.2 m) wide.[[36]](#cite_note-amny20160809-38)[[34]](#cite_note-BBPr_p._26-27-36) The iron railings were produced by [Janes & Kirtland](/wiki/Adrian_Janes "Adrian Janes"), a Bronx iron foundry that also made the [United States Capitol dome](/wiki/United_States_Capitol_dome "United States Capitol dome") and the [Bow Bridge](/wiki/Bow_Bridge_(Central_Park) "Bow Bridge (Central Park)") in [Central Park](/wiki/Central_Park "Central Park").[[37]](#cite_note-39)[[38]](#cite_note-40)

#### Approaches

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Approaches")]

Each of the side spans is reached by an approach ramp. The 971-foot (296 m) approach ramp from the Brooklyn side is shorter than the 1,567-foot (478 m) approach ramp from the Manhattan side.[[6]](#cite_note-Barnes_p._28-6) The approaches are supported by [Renaissance-style](/wiki/Renaissance_architecture "Renaissance architecture") arches made of masonry; the arch openings themselves were filled with brick walls, with small windows within.[[2]](#cite_note-NYCL-2)[[39]](#cite_note-41) The approach ramp contains nine [arch](/wiki/Arch_bridge "Arch bridge") or iron-[girder bridges](/wiki/Girder_bridge "Girder bridge") across side streets in Manhattan and Brooklyn.[[40]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._330-331-42)

[![The Brooklyn Banks, a skate park composed of brick obstacles under a series of steel viaducts](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Brooklyn_Banks_%284094206902%29.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Banks_%284094206902%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Banks_(4094206902).jpg)

[Brooklyn Banks](/wiki/Brooklyn_Banks "Brooklyn Banks") skate park, seen in 2009

Underneath the Manhattan approach, a series of brick slopes or "banks" was developed into a [skate park](/wiki/Skate_park "Skate park"), the [Brooklyn Banks](/wiki/Brooklyn_Banks "Brooklyn Banks"), in the late 1980s.[[41]](#cite_note-nyt20100513-43) The park uses the approach's support [pillars](/wiki/Pillar "Pillar") as obstacles.[[42]](#cite_note-nyt20050624-44) In the mid-2010s, the Brooklyn Banks were closed to the public because the area was being used as a storage site during the bridge's renovation.[[41]](#cite_note-nyt20100513-43) The [skateboarding](/wiki/Skateboarding "Skateboarding") community has attempted to save the banks on multiple occasions; after the city destroyed the smaller banks in the 2000s, the city government agreed to keep the larger banks for skateboarding.[[42]](#cite_note-nyt20050624-44) When the NYCDOT removed the bricks from the banks in 2020, skateboarders started an [online petition](/wiki/Online_petition "Online petition").[[43]](#cite_note-45) In the 2020s, local resident Rosa Chang advocated for the 9-acre (3.6 ha) space under the Manhattan approach to be converted into a recreational area known as Gotham Park.[[44]](#cite_note-46) Some of the space under the Manhattan approach reopened in May 2023 as a park called the Arches;[[45]](#cite_note-47) this was followed in November 2024 by another 15,000-square-foot (1,400 m2) section of parkland.[[46]](#cite_note-48)

### Cables

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Cables")]

[![A view of diagonal stays and vertical suspender cables from below. The main cables run at the top.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/USA-NYC-Brooklyn_Bridge3.jpg/250px-USA-NYC-Brooklyn_Bridge3.jpg)](/wiki/File:USA-NYC-Brooklyn_Bridge3.jpg)

View of diagonal stays and vertical suspender cables; the main cables are at top

The Brooklyn Bridge contains four main cables, which descend from the tops of the suspension towers and support the deck. Two are located to the outside of the bridge's roadways, while two are in the median of the roadways.[[7]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._29-31-7) Each main cable measures 15.75 inches (40.0 cm) in diameter and contains 5,282 parallel, [galvanized](/wiki/Galvanization "Galvanization") steel wires wrapped closely together in a cylindrical shape.[[6]](#cite_note-Barnes_p._28-6)[[47]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._350-351-49)[[48]](#cite_note-sun18910611-50) These wires are bundled in 19 individual strands, with 278 wires to a strand.[[47]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._350-351-49) This was the first use of bundling in a suspension bridge and took several months for workers to tie together.[[49]](#cite_note-nyt19291229-51) Since the 2000s, the main cables have also supported a series of 24-[watt](/wiki/Watt "Watt") [LED](/wiki/LED "LED") lighting fixtures, referred to as "necklace lights" due to their shape.[[50]](#cite_note-52)

In addition, either 1,088,[[26]](#cite_note-nyt19860308-28) 1,096,[[51]](#cite_note-n152878759-53) or 1,520 galvanized steel wire suspender cables hang downward from the main cables.[[24]](#cite_note-Talbot_p._4-26) Another 400 cable [stays](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/stay#Etymology_3 "wikt:stay") extend diagonally from the towers. The vertical suspender cables and diagonal cable stays hold up the truss structure around the bridge deck.[[24]](#cite_note-Talbot_p._4-26)[[26]](#cite_note-nyt19860308-28)[[51]](#cite_note-n152878759-53) The bridge's suspenders originally used wire rope, which was replaced in the 1980s with galvanized steel made by [Bethlehem Steel](/wiki/Bethlehem_Steel "Bethlehem Steel").[[24]](#cite_note-Talbot_p._4-26)[[52]](#cite_note-Morning_Call_m830-54) The vertical suspender cables measure 8 to 130 feet (2.4 to 39.6 m) long, and the diagonal stays measure 138 to 449 feet (42 to 137 m) long.[[51]](#cite_note-n152878759-53)

#### Anchorages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Anchorages")]

Each side of the bridge contains an anchorage for the main cables. The anchorages are [trapezoidal](/wiki/Trapezoid "Trapezoid") limestone structures located slightly inland of the shore, measuring 129 by 119 feet (39 by 36 m) at the base and 117 by 104 feet (36 by 32 m) at the top.[[5]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._2-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Barnes_p._28-6) Each anchorage weighs 60,000 short tons (54,000 long tons; 54,000 t).[[5]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._2-5) The Manhattan anchorage rests on a foundation of bedrock while the Brooklyn anchorage rests on clay.[[48]](#cite_note-sun18910611-50)

The anchorages both have four [anchor plates](/wiki/Anchor_plate "Anchor plate"), one for each of the main cables, which are located near ground level and parallel to the ground. The anchor plates measure 16 by 17.5 feet (4.9 by 5.3 m), with a thickness of 2.5 feet (0.76 m) and weigh 46,000 pounds (21,000 kg) each. Each anchor plate is connected to the respective main cable by two sets of nine [eyebars](/wiki/Eyebar "Eyebar"), each of which is about 12.5 feet (3.8 m) long and up to 9 by 3 inches (229 by 76 mm) thick.[[53]](#cite_note-55)[[54]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._5-56) The chains of eyebars curve downward from the cables toward the anchor plates, and the eyebars vary in size depending on their position.[[c]](#cite_note-57)[[40]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._330-331-42)

The anchorages also contain numerous passageways and compartments.[[55]](#cite_note-nyt19990110-58) Starting in 1876,[[56]](#cite_note-npr20170130-59) in order to fund the bridge's maintenance, the New York City government made the large vaults under the bridge's Manhattan anchorage available for rent, and they were in constant use during the early 20th century.[[55]](#cite_note-nyt19990110-58)[[57]](#cite_note-nyt19760922-60) The vaults were used to store wine, as they were kept at a consistent 60 °F (16 °C) temperature due to a lack of air circulation.[[55]](#cite_note-nyt19990110-58) The Manhattan vault was called the **"Blue Grotto"** because of a shrine to the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") next to an opening at the entrance.[[57]](#cite_note-nyt19760922-60) The vaults were closed for public use in the late 1910s and 1920s during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States") but were reopened thereafter.[[56]](#cite_note-npr20170130-59)[[57]](#cite_note-nyt19760922-60) When *[New York](/wiki/New_York_(magazine) "New York (magazine)")* magazine visited one of the cellars in 1978, it discovered a "fading inscription" on a wall reading: "Who loveth not wine, women and song, he remaineth a fool his whole life long."[[58]](#cite_note-61)[[56]](#cite_note-npr20170130-59) Leaks found within the vault's spaces necessitated repairs during the late 1980s and early 1990s.[[59]](#cite_note-62) By the late 1990s, the chambers were being used to store maintenance equipment.[[55]](#cite_note-nyt19990110-58)

### Towers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Towers")]

[![View of the pointed arches of the suspension tower from below](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9d/Standing_Tall_%282819665347%29.jpg/250px-Standing_Tall_%282819665347%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Standing_Tall_(2819665347).jpg)

Characteristic pointed arches of the bridge's [Gothic Revival](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture") suspension towers

The bridge's two suspension towers are 278 feet (85 m) tall with a footprint of 140 by 59 feet (43 by 18 m) at the high water line.[[6]](#cite_note-Barnes_p._28-6)[[3]](#cite_note-Facts_on_File_1914_p._839-3)[[7]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._29-31-7) They are built of [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite"), and [Rosendale cement](/wiki/Rosendale_cement "Rosendale cement"). The limestone was quarried at the [Clark Quarry](/wiki/Ligonier_Point_Historic_District "Ligonier Point Historic District") in [Essex County, New York](/wiki/Essex_County,_New_York "Essex County, New York").[[60]](#cite_note-63) The granite blocks were quarried and shaped on [Vinalhaven Island, Maine](/wiki/Vinalhaven,_Maine "Vinalhaven, Maine"), under a contract with the Bodwell Granite Company, and delivered from Maine to New York by schooner.[[61]](#cite_note-64) The Manhattan tower contains 46,945 cubic yards (35,892 m3) of masonry, while the Brooklyn tower has 38,214 cubic yards (29,217 m3) of masonry.[[5]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._2-5)[[6]](#cite_note-Barnes_p._28-6) There are 56 [LED](/wiki/LED "LED") lamps mounted onto the towers.[[62]](#cite_note-Simko-Bednarski_2024_a204-65)

Each tower contains a pair of [Gothic Revival](/wiki/Gothic_Revival_architecture "Gothic Revival architecture") pointed arches, through which the roadways run. The arch openings are 117 feet (36 m) tall and 33.75 feet (10.29 m) wide.[[54]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._5-56)[[63]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._564-66) The tops of the towers are located 159 feet (48 m) above the floor of each arch opening, while the floors of the openings are 119.25 feet (36.35 m) above mean water level, giving the towers a total height of 278.25 feet (84.81 m) above mean high water.[[6]](#cite_note-Barnes_p._28-6)[[63]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._564-66)

#### Caissons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Caissons")]

The towers rest on underwater [caissons](/wiki/Caisson_(engineering) "Caisson (engineering)") made of [southern yellow pine](/wiki/Southern_yellow_pine "Southern yellow pine") and filled with cement.[[6]](#cite_note-Barnes_p._28-6) Inside both caissons were spaces for construction workers. The Manhattan side's caisson is slightly larger, measuring 172 by 102 feet (52 by 31 m) and located 78.5 feet (23.9 m) below high water, while the Brooklyn side's caisson measures 168 by 102 feet (51 by 31 m) and is located 44.5 feet (13.6 m) below high water. The caissons were designed to hold at least the weight of the towers which would exert a pressure of 5 short tons per square foot (49 t/m2) when fully built, but the caissons were over-engineered for safety. During an accident on the Brooklyn side, when air pressure was lost and the partially-built towers dropped full-force down, the caisson sustained an estimated pressure of 23 short tons per square foot (220 t/m2) with only minor damage.[[6]](#cite_note-Barnes_p._28-6)[[64]](#cite_note-67) Most of the timber used in the bridge's construction, including in the caissons, came from mills at [Gascoigne Bluff](/wiki/Gascoigne_Bluff "Gascoigne Bluff") on [St. Simons Island, Georgia](/wiki/St._Simons,_Georgia "St. Simons, Georgia").[[65]](#cite_note-68)

The Brooklyn side's caisson, which was built first, originally had a height of 9.5 feet (2.9 m) and a ceiling composed of five layers of timber, each layer 1 foot (0.30 m) tall. Ten more layers of timber were later added atop the ceiling, and the entire caisson was wrapped in tin and wood for further protection against flooding. The thickness of the caisson's sides was 8 feet (2.4 m) at both the bottom and the top. The caisson had six chambers: two each for [dredging](/wiki/Dredging "Dredging"), supply shafts, and [airlocks](/wiki/Airlock "Airlock").[[66]](#cite_note-structure_mag-69)[[5]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._2-5)

The caisson on the Manhattan side was slightly different because it had to be installed at a greater depth. To protect against the increased air pressure at that depth, the Manhattan caisson had 22 layers of timber on its roof, seven more than its Brooklyn counterpart had. The Manhattan caisson also had fifty 4-inch-diameter (10 cm) pipes for sand removal, a fireproof iron-boilerplate interior, and different airlocks and communication systems.[[54]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._5-56)[[66]](#cite_note-structure_mag-69)[[67]](#cite_note-70)[[68]](#cite_note-nydh18710418-71)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: History")]

### Planning

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Planning")]

[![Early plan of one tower for the Brooklyn Bridge, drawn in 1867](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/BrooklynBridgeSchematic.jpg/250px-BrooklynBridgeSchematic.jpg)](/wiki/File:BrooklynBridgeSchematic.jpg)

Early Brooklyn Bridge tower plan, 1867

Proposals for a bridge between the then-separate cities of Brooklyn and New York had been suggested as early as 1800.[[69]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._24-25-72)[[49]](#cite_note-nyt19291229-51) At the time, the only travel between the two cities was by [a number of ferry lines](/wiki/List_of_ferries_across_the_East_River "List of ferries across the East River").[[69]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._24-25-72)[[70]](#cite_note-nyt19330521-73) Engineers presented various designs, such as chain or link bridges, though these were never built because of the difficulties of constructing a high enough fixed-span bridge across the extremely busy East River.[[69]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._24-25-72)[[49]](#cite_note-nyt19291229-51) There were also proposals for tunnels under the East River, but these were considered prohibitively expensive.[[71]](#cite_note-nyt18830524-74) German immigrant engineer John Augustus Roebling proposed building a suspension bridge over the East River in 1857. He had previously designed and constructed shorter suspension bridges, such as [Roebling's Delaware Aqueduct](/wiki/Roebling%27s_Delaware_Aqueduct "Roebling's Delaware Aqueduct") in [Lackawaxen, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Lackawaxen,_Pennsylvania "Lackawaxen, Pennsylvania"), and the Niagara Suspension Bridge. In 1867, Roebling erected what became the [John A. Roebling Suspension Bridge](/wiki/John_A._Roebling_Suspension_Bridge "John A. Roebling Suspension Bridge") over the Ohio River between [Cincinnati, Ohio](/wiki/Cincinnati,_Ohio "Cincinnati, Ohio"), and [Covington, Kentucky](/wiki/Covington,_Kentucky "Covington, Kentucky").[[72]](#cite_note-75)

In February 1867, the New York State Senate passed a bill that allowed the construction of a suspension bridge from Brooklyn to Manhattan.[[73]](#cite_note-76) Two months later, the New York and Brooklyn Bridge Company was incorporated with a board of directors (later converted to a board of trustees).[[69]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._24-25-72)[[74]](#cite_note-nybc-incorporation-77)[[75]](#cite_note-nyt18720410-78) There were twenty trustees in total: eight each appointed by the mayors of New York and Brooklyn, as well as the mayors of each city and the auditor and comptroller of Brooklyn.[[48]](#cite_note-sun18910611-50) The company was tasked with constructing what was then known as the New York and Brooklyn Bridge.[[69]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._24-25-72)[[74]](#cite_note-nybc-incorporation-77)[[75]](#cite_note-nyt18720410-78) Alternatively, the span was just referred to as the "Brooklyn Bridge", a name originating in a January 25, 1867, [letter to the editor](/wiki/Letter_to_the_editor "Letter to the editor") sent to the *[Brooklyn Daily Eagle](/wiki/Brooklyn_Eagle "Brooklyn Eagle").*[[76]](#cite_note-79) The act of incorporation, which became law on April 16, 1867, authorized the cities of New York (now Manhattan) and Brooklyn to subscribe to $5 million in [capital stock](/wiki/Capital_stock "Capital stock"), which would fund the bridge's construction.[[71]](#cite_note-nyt18830524-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/The_great_East_River_bridge-_to_connect_the_cities_of_New_York_%26_Brooklyn_LCCN2001704255_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-The_great_East_River_bridge-_to_connect_the_cities_of_New_York_%26_Brooklyn_LCCN2001704255_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_great_East_River_bridge-_to_connect_the_cities_of_New_York_%26_Brooklyn_LCCN2001704255_(cropped).jpg)

Artists' conception, by [Currier and Ives](/wiki/Currier_and_Ives "Currier and Ives"), of the bridge while construction was underway, 1872

Roebling was subsequently named the chief engineer of the work and, by September 1867, had presented a master plan.[[69]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._24-25-72)[[77]](#cite_note-80)[[78]](#cite_note-81) According to the plan, the bridge would be longer and taller than any suspension bridge previously built.[[7]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._29-31-7) It would incorporate roadways and [elevated rail](/wiki/Elevated_railway "Elevated railway") tracks, whose tolls and fares would provide the means to pay for the bridge's construction. It would also include a raised promenade that served as a leisurely pathway.[[79]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._32-33-82) The proposal received much acclaim in both cities, and residents predicted that the New York and Brooklyn Bridge's opening would have as much of an impact as the [Suez Canal](/wiki/Suez_Canal "Suez Canal"), the first [transatlantic telegraph cable](/wiki/Transatlantic_telegraph_cable "Transatlantic telegraph cable") or the [first transcontinental railroad](/wiki/First_transcontinental_railroad "First transcontinental railroad"). By early 1869, however, some individuals started to criticize the project, saying either that the bridge was too expensive, or that the construction process was too difficult.[[80]](#cite_note-83)

To allay concerns about the design of the New York and Brooklyn Bridge, Roebling set up a "Bridge Party" in March 1869, where he invited engineers and members of U.S. Congress to see his other spans.[[81]](#cite_note-84) Following the bridge party in April, Roebling and several engineers conducted final surveys. During the process, it was determined that the main span would have to be raised from 130 to 135 feet (40 to 41 m) above MHW, requiring several changes to the overall design.[[82]](#cite_note-85) In June 1869, while conducting these surveys, Roebling sustained a crush injury to his foot when a [ferry](/wiki/Ferry "Ferry") pinned it against a [piling](/wiki/Piling "Piling").[[83]](#cite_note-86)[[84]](#cite_note-87) After amputation of his crushed toes, he developed a [tetanus](/wiki/Tetanus "Tetanus") infection that left him incapacitated and resulted in his death the following month. [Washington Roebling](/wiki/Washington_Roebling "Washington Roebling"), John Roebling's 32-year-old son, was then hired to fill his father's role.[[85]](#cite_note-88)[[86]](#cite_note-89) [Tammany Hall](/wiki/Tammany_Hall "Tammany Hall") leader [William M. Tweed](/wiki/William_M._Tweed "William M. Tweed") also became involved in the bridge's construction because, as a major landowner in New York City, he had an interest in the project's completion.[[87]](#cite_note-90) The New York and Brooklyn Bridge Company—later known simply as the New York Bridge Company[[88]](#cite_note-91)—was actually overseen by Tammany Hall, and it approved Roebling's plans and designated him as chief engineer of the project.[[89]](#cite_note-92)

### Construction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Construction")]

#### Caissons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Caissons")]

[![Woodcut/drawing illustrating caisson used in the construction of the Brooklyn Bridge](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ef/AmCyc_Caisson_-_Caisson_of_East_River_Bridge.jpg/250px-AmCyc_Caisson_-_Caisson_of_East_River_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:AmCyc_Caisson_-_Caisson_of_East_River_Bridge.jpg)

Diagram of the caisson

Construction of the Brooklyn Bridge began on January 2, 1870.[[48]](#cite_note-sun18910611-50) The first work entailed the construction of two caissons, upon which the suspension towers would be built.[[66]](#cite_note-structure_mag-69)[[5]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._2-5) The Brooklyn side's caisson was built at the Webb & Bell shipyard in [Greenpoint, Brooklyn](/wiki/Greenpoint,_Brooklyn "Greenpoint, Brooklyn"), and was launched into the river on March 19, 1870.[[66]](#cite_note-structure_mag-69)[[5]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._2-5) Compressed air was pumped into the caisson, and workers entered the space to dig the sediment until it sank to the bedrock. As one sixteen-year-old from Ireland, [Frank Harris](/wiki/Frank_Harris "Frank Harris"), described the fearful experience:[[90]](#cite_note-:02-93)

> The six of us were working naked to the waist in the small iron chamber with the temperature of about 80 degrees Fahrenheit: In five minutes the sweat was pouring from us, and all the while we were standing in icy water that was only kept from rising by the terrific pressure. No wonder the headaches were blinding.[[90]](#cite_note-:02-93)

Once the caisson had reached the desired depth, it was to be filled in with vertical brick [piers](/wiki/Pier_(architecture) "Pier (architecture)") and concrete.[[91]](#cite_note-94)[[92]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._196-95) However, due to the unexpectedly high concentration of large boulders atop the riverbed, the Brooklyn caisson took several months to sink to the desired depth.[[92]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._196-95)[[5]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._2-5) Furthermore, in December 1870, its timber roof caught fire, delaying construction further.[[93]](#cite_note-96)[[94]](#cite_note-97) The "Great Blowout", as the fire was called, delayed construction for several months, since the holes in the caisson had to be repaired.[[95]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._242-245-98) On March 6, 1871, the repairs were finished, and the caisson had reached its final depth of 44.5 feet (13.6 m); it was filled with concrete five days later.[[95]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._242-245-98)[[5]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._2-5) Overall, about 264 individuals were estimated to have worked in the caisson every day, but because of high worker [turnover](/wiki/Turnover_(employment) "Turnover (employment)"), the final total was thought to be about 2,500 men in total.[[96]](#cite_note-99) Only a few workers were paralyzed by [decompression sickness](/wiki/Decompression_sickness "Decompression sickness"); at its final depth, the caisson's air pressure was 21 pounds per square inch (140 kPa).[[97]](#cite_note-Talbot_p._3-100)

The Manhattan side's caisson was the next structure to be built. To ensure that it would not catch fire like its counterpart had, the Manhattan caisson was lined with fireproof plate iron.[[68]](#cite_note-nydh18710418-71) It was launched from Webb & Bell's shipyard on May 11, 1871,[[98]](#cite_note-101)[[54]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._5-56) and maneuvered into place that September.[[99]](#cite_note-102)[[66]](#cite_note-structure_mag-69) Due to the extreme underwater air pressure inside the much deeper Manhattan caisson, many workers became sick with "the bends"—decompression sickness—during this work,[[90]](#cite_note-:02-93) despite the incorporation of airlocks (which were believed to help with decompression sickness at the time).[[100]](#cite_note-Hudson-103)[[101]](#cite_note-104) This condition was unknown at the time and was first called "caisson disease" by the project physician, Andrew Smith.[[102]](#cite_note-105)[[103]](#cite_note-106) Between January 25 and May 31, 1872, Smith treated 110 cases of decompression sickness, while three workers died from the disease.[[54]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._5-56) When iron probes underneath the Manhattan caisson found the [bedrock](/wiki/Bedrock "Bedrock") to be even deeper than expected, Washington Roebling halted construction due to the increased risk of decompression sickness.[[54]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._5-56)[[104]](#cite_note-107) After the Manhattan caisson reached a depth of 78.5 feet (23.9 m) with an air pressure of 35 pounds per square inch (240 [kPa](/wiki/Pascal_(unit) "Pascal (unit)")),[[97]](#cite_note-Talbot_p._3-100) Washington deemed the sandy subsoil overlying the bedrock 30 feet (9.1 m) beneath to be sufficiently firm, and subsequently infilled the caisson with concrete in July 1872.[[54]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._5-56)[[105]](#cite_note-108)

Washington Roebling himself suffered a paralyzing injury as a result of caisson disease shortly after ground was broken for the Brooklyn tower foundation.[[49]](#cite_note-nyt19291229-51)[[106]](#cite_note-109) His debilitating condition left him unable to supervise the construction in person, so he designed the caissons and other equipment from his apartment, directing "the completion of the bridge through a telescope from his bedroom."[[90]](#cite_note-:02-93) His wife, [Emily Warren Roebling](/wiki/Emily_Warren_Roebling "Emily Warren Roebling"), not only provided written communications between her husband and the engineers on site,[[107]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._297-298-110) but also understood [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), calculations of [catenary](/wiki/Catenary "Catenary") curves, strengths of materials, bridge specifications, and the intricacies of cable construction. She spent the next 11 years helping supervise the bridge's construction,[[97]](#cite_note-Talbot_p._3-100)[[108]](#cite_note-111) taking over much of the chief engineer's duties, including day-to-day supervision and project management.[[109]](#cite_note-112)

#### Towers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Towers")]

[![View of Manhattan in 1876, showing the Brooklyn Bridge under construction](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/Manhattan_1876.jpg/330px-Manhattan_1876.jpg)](/wiki/File:Manhattan_1876.jpg)

View of Manhattan in 1876, showing the Brooklyn Bridge under construction

After the caissons were completed, piers were constructed on top of each of them upon which masonry towers would be built. The towers' construction was a complex process that took four years. Since the masonry blocks were heavy, the builders transported them to the base of the towers using a [pulley](/wiki/Pulley "Pulley") system with a continuous 1.5-inch-diameter (3.8 cm) steel wire rope, operated by steam engines at ground level. The blocks were then carried up on a timber track alongside each tower and maneuvered into the proper position using a [derrick](/wiki/Derrick "Derrick") atop the towers.[[54]](#cite_note-NRHI_Nomination_Form_p._5-56)[[110]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._333-113) The blocks sometimes vibrated the ropes because of their weight, but only once did a block fall.[[110]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._333-113)

Construction on the suspension towers started in mid-1872, and by the time work was halted for the winter in late 1872, parts of each tower had already been built.[[107]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._297-298-110) By mid-1873, there was substantial progress on the towers' construction. The Brooklyn side's tower had reached a height of 164 feet (50 m) above mean high water (MHW), while the tower on the Manhattan side had reached 88 feet (27 m) above MHW.[[111]](#cite_note-114)[[112]](#cite_note-115) The arches of the Brooklyn tower were completed by August 1874.[[113]](#cite_note-116) The tower was substantially finished by December 1874 with the erection of saddle plates for the main cables at the top of the tower. However, the ornamentation on the Brooklyn tower could not be completed until the Manhattan tower was finished.[[114]](#cite_note-117) The last stone on the Brooklyn tower was raised in June 1875 and the Manhattan tower was completed in July 1876.[[115]](#cite_note-118) The saddle plates atop both towers were also raised in July 1876.[[116]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._338-119) The work was dangerous: by 1876, three workers had died having fallen from the towers, while nine other workers were killed in other accidents.[[117]](#cite_note-120)

When the towers were completed, they were taller than any building in the city, with the exception of the spire of [Trinity Church](/wiki/Trinity_Church_(Manhattan) "Trinity Church (Manhattan)"), which reached 279 feet (85 m). This remained the case until 1890.[[118]](#cite_note-121)

[![George Bradford Brainerd's painting "From Bridge Tower", circa 1872](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Brainerd%2C_From_Bridge_Tower._1996.164.2-1425.jpg/250px-Brainerd%2C_From_Bridge_Tower._1996.164.2-1425.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brainerd,_From_Bridge_Tower._1996.164.2-1425.jpg)

[George Bradford Brainerd](/wiki/George_Bradford_Brainerd "George Bradford Brainerd"), *From Bridge Tower*, c. 1872, [Brooklyn Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Museum "Brooklyn Museum")

In 1875, while the towers were being constructed, the project had depleted its original $5 million budget. Two bridge commissioners, one each from Brooklyn and Manhattan, petitioned New York state lawmakers to allot another $8 million for construction. Ultimately, the legislators passed a law authorizing the allotment with the condition that the cities would buy the stock of Brooklyn Bridge's private stockholders.[[119]](#cite_note-122)

Work proceeded concurrently on the anchorages on each side.[[120]](#cite_note-123) The Brooklyn anchorage broke ground in January 1873[[121]](#cite_note-124)[[40]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._330-331-42) and was subsequently substantially completed in August 1875.[[122]](#cite_note-125)[[40]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._330-331-42) The Manhattan anchorage was built in less time, having started in May 1875, it was mostly completed in July 1876.[[123]](#cite_note-126)[[124]](#cite_note-127) The anchorages could not be fully completed until the main cables were spun, at which point another 6 feet (1.8 m) would be added to the height of each 80-foot (24 m) anchorage.[[125]](#cite_note-nyt18770911-128)

#### Cables

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Cables")]

The first temporary wire was stretched between the towers on August 15, 1876,[[116]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._338-119)[[126]](#cite_note-129)[[127]](#cite_note-bde18760811-130) using [chrome steel](/wiki/Chrome_steel "Chrome steel") provided by the Chrome Steel Company of Brooklyn.[[116]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._338-119)[[127]](#cite_note-bde18760811-130) The wire was then stretched back across the river, and the two ends were [spliced](/wiki/Rope_splicing "Rope splicing") to form a traveler, a lengthy loop of wire connecting the towers, which was driven by a 30 horsepower (22 kW) steam hoisting engine at ground level.[[128]](#cite_note-131) The wire was one of two that were used to create a temporary footbridge for workers while cable spinning was ongoing.[[129]](#cite_note-nyt18760830-132) The next step was to send an engineer across the completed traveler wire in a [boatswain's chair](/wiki/Bosun%27s_chair "Bosun's chair") slung from the wire, to show the workforce it was safe enough. The bridge's master mechanic, E. F. Farrington, was selected for this task, and an estimated crowd of 10,000 people on both shores watched him cross.[[130]](#cite_note-133)[[131]](#cite_note-134) A second traveler wire was then stretched across the span, a task that was completed by August 30.[[132]](#cite_note-135)[[129]](#cite_note-nyt18760830-132) The temporary footbridge, located some 60 feet (18 m) above the elevation of the future deck, was completed in February 1877.[[133]](#cite_note-136)

[![Under construction, circa 1872–1887](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/ca/Construction_of_Brooklyn_Bridge%2C_ca._1872-1887._%285832930865%29.jpg/250px-Construction_of_Brooklyn_Bridge%2C_ca._1872-1887._%285832930865%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Construction_of_Brooklyn_Bridge,_ca._1872-1887._(5832930865).jpg)

Under construction, c. 1872 – c. 1877

[!["From Tower to Tower—the suspension bridge over the East River—view from the Brooklyn Tower", an 1877 artwork](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/View_from_Tower_to_Tower%2C_1877.jpg/250px-View_from_Tower_to_Tower%2C_1877.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_from_Tower_to_Tower,_1877.jpg)

"From Tower to Tower—the suspension bridge over the East River—view from the Brooklyn Tower" (1877)

By December 1876, a steel contract for the permanent cables still had not been awarded.[[134]](#cite_note-nyt18761228-137) There was disagreement over whether the bridge's cables should use the as-yet-untested [Bessemer steel](/wiki/Bessemer_steel "Bessemer steel") or the well-proven [crucible steel](/wiki/Crucible_steel "Crucible steel").[[24]](#cite_note-Talbot_p._4-26)[[135]](#cite_note-138) Until a permanent contract was awarded, the builders ordered 30 short tons (27 long tons) of wire in the interim, 10 tons each from three companies, including [Washington Roebling's own steel mill](/wiki/John_A._Roebling%27s_Sons_Company,_Trenton_N.J.,_Block_3 "John A. Roebling's Sons Company, Trenton N.J., Block 3") in [Trenton, New Jersey](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey").[[136]](#cite_note-139) In the end, it was decided to use number 8 [Birmingham gauge](/wiki/Birmingham_gauge "Birmingham gauge") (approximately 4 mm or 0.165 inches in diameter) crucible steel, and a request for bids was distributed, to which eight companies responded.[[134]](#cite_note-nyt18761228-137) In January 1877, a contract for crucible steel was awarded to [J. Lloyd Haigh](/w/index.php?title=J._Lloyd_Haigh&action=edit&redlink=1 "J. Lloyd Haigh (page does not exist)"),[[24]](#cite_note-Talbot_p._4-26)[[137]](#cite_note-140)[[138]](#cite_note-141) who was associated with bridge trustee [Abram Hewitt](/wiki/Abram_Hewitt "Abram Hewitt"), whom Roebling distrusted.[[26]](#cite_note-nyt19860308-28)[[139]](#cite_note-142)

The spinning of the wires required the manufacture of large coils of it which were galvanized but not oiled when they left the factory. The coils were delivered to a yard near the Brooklyn anchorage. There they were dipped in [linseed oil](/wiki/Linseed_oil "Linseed oil"), hoisted to the top of the anchorage, dried out and spliced into a single wire, and finally coated with red zinc for further galvanizing.[[140]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._400-401-143)[[141]](#cite_note-144) There were thirty-two drums at the anchorage yard, eight for each of the four main cables. Each drum had a capacity of 60,000 feet (18,000 m) of wire.[[142]](#cite_note-145) The first experimental wire for the main cables was stretched between the towers on May 29, 1877, and spinning began two weeks later.[[140]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._400-401-143) All four main cables were being strung by that July. During that time, the temporary footbridge was unofficially opened to members of the public, who could receive a visitor's pass; by August 1877 several thousand visitors from around the world had used the footbridge.[[143]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._405-408-146) The visitor passes ceased that September after a visitor had an [epileptic seizure](/wiki/Epileptic_seizure "Epileptic seizure") and nearly fell off.[[143]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._405-408-146)[[144]](#cite_note-bde18771002-147)

As the wires were being spun, work also commenced on the demolition of buildings on either side of the river for the Brooklyn Bridge's approaches; this work was mostly complete by September 1877.[[125]](#cite_note-nyt18770911-128) The following month, initial contracts were awarded for the suspender wires, which would hang down from the main cables and support the deck.[[144]](#cite_note-bde18771002-147)[[145]](#cite_note-148) By May 1878, the main cables were more than two-thirds complete.[[146]](#cite_note-149) However, the following month, one of the wires slipped, killing two people and injuring three others.[[147]](#cite_note-150)[[148]](#cite_note-151)[[149]](#cite_note-152) In 1877, Hewitt wrote a letter urging against the use of Bessemer steel in the bridge's construction.[[150]](#cite_note-153)[[151]](#cite_note-154) Bids had been submitted for both crucible steel and Bessemer steel; [John A. Roebling's Sons](/w/index.php?title=John_A._Roebling%27s_Sons&action=edit&redlink=1 "John A. Roebling's Sons (page does not exist)") submitted the lowest bid for Bessemer steel,[[152]](#cite_note-155) but at Hewitt's direction, the contract was awarded to Haigh.[[153]](#cite_note-156)[[154]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._443-444-157)

A subsequent wire sampling by Roebling's assistant engineers discovered that Haigh had substituted inferior quality wire in the cables. Of eighty rings of wire that were tested, only five met standards, and it was estimated that Haigh had earned $300,000 from the deception.[[154]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._443-444-157)[[155]](#cite_note-Mensch_2018_p._114-158) At this point, it was too late to replace the cables that had already been constructed. Roebling determined that the poorer wire would leave the bridge only four times as strong as necessary, rather than six to eight times as strong. The inferior-quality wire was allowed to remain and 150 extra wires were added to each cable.[[24]](#cite_note-Talbot_p._4-26)[[154]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._443-444-157)[[155]](#cite_note-Mensch_2018_p._114-158) To avoid public controversy, Haigh was not fired, but instead was required to personally pay for the extra higher-quality wire needed.[[155]](#cite_note-Mensch_2018_p._114-158)[[d]](#cite_note-159) The contract for the remaining wire was awarded to the John A. Roebling's Sons, and by October 5, 1878, the last of the main cables' wires went over the river.[[156]](#cite_note-160)

#### Nearing completion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Nearing completion")]

[![A colored lithograph of the completed Brooklyn Bridge looking towards Manhattan](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Currier_and_Ives_Brooklyn_Bridge2.jpg/250px-Currier_and_Ives_Brooklyn_Bridge2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Currier_and_Ives_Brooklyn_Bridge2.jpg)

[Chromolithograph](/wiki/Chromolithograph "Chromolithograph") of the "Great East River Suspension Bridge" by Currier and Ives, created in 1883.

[![A photograph of the bridge from a similar angle as the lithograph, with the skyscrapers of Lower Manhattan in view](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/View_from_the_Manhattan_Bridge_008.jpg/250px-View_from_the_Manhattan_Bridge_008.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_from_the_Manhattan_Bridge_008.jpg)

Similar view, 2023

After the suspender wires had been placed, workers began erecting steel crossbeams to support the roadway as part of the bridge's overall superstructure.[[24]](#cite_note-Talbot_p._4-26) Construction on the bridge's superstructure started in March 1879,[[29]](#cite_note-concise-description-31) but, as with the cables, the trustees initially disagreed on whether the steel superstructure should be made of Bessemer or crucible steel.[[157]](#cite_note-161) That July, the trustees decided to award a contract for 500 short tons (450 long tons) of Bessemer steel[[158]](#cite_note-gazette18800412-162) to the Edgemoor (or Edge Moor) Iron Works, based in [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia"), to be delivered by 1880.[[158]](#cite_note-gazette18800412-162)[[159]](#cite_note-163)[[160]](#cite_note-164) The trustees later passed another resolution for another 500 short tons (450 long tons) of Bessemer steel.[[158]](#cite_note-gazette18800412-162) However, by February 1880 the steel deliveries had not started.[[161]](#cite_note-165) That October, the bridge trustees questioned Edgemoor's president about the delay in steel deliveries.[[162]](#cite_note-166) Despite Edgemoor's assurances that the contract would be fulfilled, the deliveries still had not been completed by November 1881.[[163]](#cite_note-167) Brooklyn mayor [Seth Low](/wiki/Seth_Low "Seth Low"), who became part of the board of trustees in 1882, became the chairman of a committee tasked to investigate Edgemoor's failure to fulfill the contract. When questioned, Edgemoor's president stated that the delays were the fault of another contractor, the [Cambria Iron Company](/wiki/Cambria_Iron_Company "Cambria Iron Company"), who was manufacturing the eyebars for the bridge trusses; at that point, the contract was supposed to be complete by October 1882.[[164]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._485-168)[[165]](#cite_note-169)

Further complicating the situation, Washington Roebling had failed to appear at the trustees' meeting in June 1882, since he had gone to [Newport, Rhode Island](/wiki/Newport,_Rhode_Island "Newport, Rhode Island").[[164]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._485-168) After the news media discovered this, most of the newspapers called for Roebling to be fired as chief engineer, except for the *Daily State Gazette* of [Trenton, New Jersey](/wiki/Trenton,_New_Jersey "Trenton, New Jersey"), and the *Brooklyn Daily Eagle*.[[166]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._494-495-170) Some of the longstanding trustees, including [Henry C. Murphy](/wiki/Henry_C._Murphy "Henry C. Murphy"), [James S. T. Stranahan](/wiki/James_S._T._Stranahan "James S. T. Stranahan"), and [William C. Kingsley](/wiki/William_C._Kingsley "William C. Kingsley"), were willing to vouch for Roebling, since construction progress on the Brooklyn Bridge was still ongoing. However, Roebling's behavior was considered suspect among the younger trustees who had joined the board more recently.[[166]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._494-495-170)

Construction on the bridge itself was noted in formal reports that Murphy presented each month to the mayors of New York and Brooklyn. For example, Murphy's report in August 1882 noted that the month's progress included 114 intermediate cords erected within a week, as well as 72 diagonal stays, 60 posts, and numerous floor beams, bridging trusses, and stay bars.[[166]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._494-495-170)[[167]](#cite_note-171) By early 1883, the Brooklyn Bridge was considered mostly completed and was projected to open that June.[[168]](#cite_note-nyt18830213-172) Contracts for bridge lighting were awarded by February 1883,[[168]](#cite_note-nyt18830213-172) and a toll scheme was approved that March.[[169]](#cite_note-nyt18830313-173)

#### Opposition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Opposition")]

There was substantial opposition to the bridge's construction from shipbuilders and merchants located to the north, who argued that the bridge would not provide sufficient clearance underneath for ships.[[170]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._424-174) In May 1876, these groups, led by Abraham Miller, filed a lawsuit in the [United States District Court for the Southern District of New York](/wiki/United_States_District_Court_for_the_Southern_District_of_New_York "United States District Court for the Southern District of New York") against the cities of New York and Brooklyn.[[170]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._424-174)[[171]](#cite_note-175)

In 1879, an Assembly Sub-Committee on Commerce and Navigation began an investigation into the Brooklyn Bridge. A seaman who had been hired to determine the height of the span, testified to the committee about the difficulties that ship masters would experience in bringing their ships under the bridge when it was completed. Another witness, [Edward Wellman Serrell](/wiki/Edward_Wellman_Serrell "Edward Wellman Serrell"), a civil engineer, said that the calculations of the bridge's assumed strength were incorrect.[[172]](#cite_note-176)[[173]](#cite_note-177) The Supreme Court decided in 1883 that the Brooklyn Bridge was a lawful structure.[[174]](#cite_note-178)

### Opening

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Opening")]

[![Newspaper headline announcing the Brooklyn Bridge's opening](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/28/1883_Frank_Leslie%27s_Illustrated_Newspaper_Brooklyn_Bridge_New_York_City.jpg/250px-1883_Frank_Leslie%27s_Illustrated_Newspaper_Brooklyn_Bridge_New_York_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:1883_Frank_Leslie%27s_Illustrated_Newspaper_Brooklyn_Bridge_New_York_City.jpg)

Newspaper headline announcing the Brooklyn Bridge's opening

The New York and Brooklyn Bridge was opened for use on May 24, 1883. Thousands of people attended the opening ceremony, and many ships were present in the East River for the occasion.[[175]](#cite_note-179) Officially, Emily Warren Roebling was the first to cross the bridge.[[176]](#cite_note-bpl-timeline-180) The bridge opening was also attended by U.S. president [Chester A. Arthur](/wiki/Chester_A._Arthur "Chester A. Arthur") and New York mayor [Franklin Edson](/wiki/Franklin_Edson "Franklin Edson"), who crossed the bridge and shook hands with Brooklyn mayor Seth Low at the Brooklyn end.[[177]](#cite_note-Reeves_pp._359-360-181) Abram Hewitt gave the principal address.[[178]](#cite_note-182)[[179]](#cite_note-183)

> It is not the work of any one man or of any one age. It is the result of the study, of the experience, and of the knowledge of many men in many ages. It is not merely a creation; it is a growth. It stands before us today as the sum and epitome of human knowledge; as the very heir of the ages; as the latest glory of centuries of patient observation, profound study and accumulated skill, gained, step by step, in the never-ending struggle of man to subdue the forces of nature to his control and use.

— Abram Hewitt[[180]](#cite_note-184)

Though Washington Roebling was unable to attend the ceremony (and rarely visited the site again), he held a celebratory banquet at his house on the day of the bridge opening. Further festivity included the performance by a band, gunfire from ships, and a fireworks display.[[177]](#cite_note-Reeves_pp._359-360-181) On that first day, a total of 1,800 vehicles and 150,300 people crossed the span.[[176]](#cite_note-bpl-timeline-180) Less than a week after the Brooklyn Bridge opened, ferry crews reported a sharp drop in patronage, while the bridge's toll operators were processing over a hundred people a minute.[[181]](#cite_note-185) However, cross-river ferries continued to operate until 1942.[[182]](#cite_note-bc18830531-186)

[![An image titled "Bird's-Eye View of the Great New York and Brooklyn Bridge and Grand Display of Fire Works on Opening Night"](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Brooklyn_Museum_-_Bird%27s-Eye_View_of_the_Great_New_York_and_Brooklyn_Bridge_and_Grand_Display_of_Fire_Works_on_Opening_Night.jpg/330px-Brooklyn_Museum_-_Bird%27s-Eye_View_of_the_Great_New_York_and_Brooklyn_Bridge_and_Grand_Display_of_Fire_Works_on_Opening_Night.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Museum_-_Bird%27s-Eye_View_of_the_Great_New_York_and_Brooklyn_Bridge_and_Grand_Display_of_Fire_Works_on_Opening_Night.jpg)

"Bird's-Eye View of the Great New York and Brooklyn Bridge and Grand Display of Fire Works on Opening Night"

The bridge had cost US$15.5 million in 1883 dollars (about US$518,304,000 in 2025[[183]](#cite_note-inflation-US-187)) to build, of which Brooklyn paid two-thirds.[[176]](#cite_note-bpl-timeline-180)[[48]](#cite_note-sun18910611-50) The bonds to fund the construction would not be paid off until 1956.[[184]](#cite_note-188) An estimated 27 men died during its construction.[[176]](#cite_note-bpl-timeline-180)[[48]](#cite_note-sun18910611-50) Since the New York and Brooklyn Bridge was the only bridge across the East River at that time, it was also called the East River Bridge.[[185]](#cite_note-189) Until the construction of the nearby [Williamsburg Bridge](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge") in 1903, the New York and Brooklyn Bridge was the longest suspension bridge in the world,[[186]](#cite_note-190) 20% longer than any built previously.[[187]](#cite_note-NiagaraCliftonBridge-191)

At the time of opening, the Brooklyn Bridge was not complete; the proposed public transit across the bridge was still being tested, while the Brooklyn approach was being completed.[[188]](#cite_note-192) On May 30, 1883, six days after the opening, a woman falling down a stairway at the Brooklyn approach caused a stampede which resulted in at least [twelve people being crushed and killed](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge_stampede "Brooklyn Bridge stampede").[[189]](#cite_note-193)[[182]](#cite_note-bc18830531-186) In subsequent lawsuits, the Brooklyn Bridge Company was acquitted of negligence.[[182]](#cite_note-bc18830531-186) However, the company did install emergency phone boxes and additional railings,[[190]](#cite_note-194) and the trustees approved a fireproofing plan for the bridge.[[191]](#cite_note-195) Public transit service began with the opening of the [New York and Brooklyn Bridge Railway](/wiki/New_York_and_Brooklyn_Bridge_Railway "New York and Brooklyn Bridge Railway"), a cable car service, on September 25, 1883.[[182]](#cite_note-bc18830531-186)[[192]](#cite_note-nyt18830925-196)[[193]](#cite_note-Small_p._5-197) On May 17, 1884, one of the circus master [P. T. Barnum](/wiki/P._T._Barnum "P. T. Barnum")'s most famous attractions, [Jumbo](/wiki/Jumbo "Jumbo") the elephant, led a parade of 21 elephants over the Brooklyn Bridge. This helped to lessen doubts about the bridge's stability while also promoting Barnum's circus.[[8]](#cite_note-tribune19220729-9)[[194]](#cite_note-198)[[195]](#cite_note-199)[[196]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._546-547-200)

### 1880s to 1900s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: 1880s to 1900s")]

[![A black-and-white view of the Brooklyn Bridge in 1899 looking east on the pedestrian walkway](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ab/Brooklyn_Bridge%2C_Looking_East%2C_New_York_City_Side%2C_July_7%2C_1899._%285833478432%29.jpg/120px-Brooklyn_Bridge%2C_Looking_East%2C_New_York_City_Side%2C_July_7%2C_1899._%285833478432%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Bridge,_Looking_East,_New_York_City_Side,_July_7,_1899._(5833478432).jpg)

Eastward view in 1899

[![A grayscale view of the bridge approach on Brooklyn side circa 1899](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Brooklyn_Bridge_Entrance_on_Brooklyn_side%2C_1899.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Bridge_Entrance_on_Brooklyn_side%2C_1899.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Bridge_Entrance_on_Brooklyn_side,_1899.jpg)

Bridge approach on Brooklyn side c. 1899

[![A black-and-white view of the bridge promenade and terminal, Manhattan side, circa 1899](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/Brooklyn_Bridge_NY_Terminal_view_from_Promenade.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Brooklyn_Bridge_NY_Terminal_view_from_Promenade.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Bridge_NY_Terminal_view_from_Promenade.tif)

Bridge Promenade and Terminal, Manhattan side, c. 1899

[![A color view of the bridge entrance on the Manhattan side, circa 1911](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Brooklyn_Bridge_Entrance_on_Park_Row.jpg/330px-Brooklyn_Bridge_Entrance_on_Park_Row.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Bridge_Entrance_on_Park_Row.jpg)

Bridge entrance on Manhattan side, c. 1911

Patronage across the Brooklyn Bridge increased in the years after it opened; a million people paid to cross in the first six months. The bridge carried 8.5 million people in 1884, its first full year of operation; this number doubled to 17 million in 1885 and again to 34 million in 1889.[[48]](#cite_note-sun18910611-50) Many of these people were cable car passengers.[[197]](#cite_note-Small_pp._10-11-201) Additionally, about 4.5 million pedestrians a year were crossing the bridge for free by 1892.[[198]](#cite_note-202)

The first proposal to make changes to the bridge was sent in only two and a half years after it opened, when [Linda Gilbert](/wiki/Linda_Gilbert "Linda Gilbert") suggested glass steam-powered elevators and an observatory be added to the bridge and a fee charged for use, which would in part fund the bridge's upkeep and in part fund her prison reform charity.[[199]](#cite_note-:0-203) This proposal was considered but not acted upon. Numerous other proposals were made during the first fifty years of the bridge's life.[[199]](#cite_note-:0-203) [Trolley](/wiki/Tram "Tram") tracks were added in the center lanes of both roadways in 1898, allowing trolleys to use the bridge as well. That year, the formerly separate City of Brooklyn was [unified with New York City](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York "City of Greater New York"), and the Brooklyn Bridge fell under city control.[[200]](#cite_note-Small_p._14-204)[[201]](#cite_note-nyt18980217-205)

Concerns about the Brooklyn Bridge's safety were raised during the turn of the century. In 1898, traffic backups due to a dead horse caused one of the truss cords to buckle.[[202]](#cite_note-nyt19020316-206) There were more significant worries after twelve suspender cables snapped in 1901,[[8]](#cite_note-tribune19220729-9)[[203]](#cite_note-207)[[204]](#cite_note-208) though a thorough investigation found no other defects.[[205]](#cite_note-209) After the 1901 incident, five inspectors were hired to examine the bridge each day, a service that cost $250,000 a year.[[206]](#cite_note-210) The [Brooklyn Rapid Transit Company](/wiki/Brooklyn_Rapid_Transit_Company "Brooklyn Rapid Transit Company"), which operated routes across the Brooklyn Bridge, issued a notice in 1905 saying that the bridge had reached its transit capacity.[[8]](#cite_note-tribune19220729-9)

By 1890, due to the popularity of the Brooklyn Bridge, there were proposals to construct other bridges across the East River between Manhattan and [Long Island](/wiki/Long_Island "Long Island").[[207]](#cite_note-211) Although a second deck for the Brooklyn Bridge was proposed, it was thought to be infeasible because doing so would overload the bridge's structural capacity.[[202]](#cite_note-nyt19020316-206) The first new bridge across the East River, the Williamsburg Bridge, opened upstream in 1903 and connected [Williamsburg, Brooklyn](/wiki/Williamsburg,_Brooklyn "Williamsburg, Brooklyn"), with the [Lower East Side](/wiki/Lower_East_Side "Lower East Side") of Manhattan.[[208]](#cite_note-212) This was followed by the [Queensboro Bridge](/wiki/Queensboro_Bridge "Queensboro Bridge") between Queens and Manhattan in March 1909,[[209]](#cite_note-213) and the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") between Brooklyn and Manhattan in December 1909.[[210]](#cite_note-214) Several subway, railroad, and road tunnels were also constructed, which helped to accelerate the development of Manhattan, Brooklyn, and Queens.[[211]](#cite_note-215)[[70]](#cite_note-nyt19330521-73)

### 1910s to 1940s

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: 1910s to 1940s")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/NYC_Manhattan_1938_Franz_Grasser.jpg/250px-NYC_Manhattan_1938_Franz_Grasser.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYC_Manhattan_1938_Franz_Grasser.jpg)

The main steel cables of the Brooklyn Bridge are visible on the left side of this photo in 1938.

Tolls on the bridge were abolished in 1911.[[212]](#cite_note-nyt19110719-216)[[213]](#cite_note-n33421526-217) In addition, the city government passed a bill to officially name the structure the "Brooklyn Bridge" in January 1915.[[214]](#cite_note-brooklyn-by-name-218)

Ostensibly in an attempt to reduce traffic on nearby city streets, [Grover Whalen](/wiki/Grover_Whalen "Grover Whalen"), the commissioner of Plant and Structures, banned motor vehicles from the Brooklyn Bridge on July 6, 1922.[[215]](#cite_note-nyt19220707-219) The real reason for the ban was an incident the same year where two cables slipped due to high traffic loads.[[8]](#cite_note-tribune19220729-9)[[216]](#cite_note-nyt19220729-220) Both Whalen and Roebling called for the renovation of the Brooklyn Bridge and the construction of a parallel bridge, though the parallel bridge was never built.[[8]](#cite_note-tribune19220729-9)[[216]](#cite_note-nyt19220729-220)[[217]](#cite_note-221) Whalen's successor William Wirt Mills announced in 1924 that a new wood-block pavement would be installed, permitting motor vehicles to use the bridge again;[[218]](#cite_note-p511453785-222)[[219]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1924_p079-223) motor traffic was again allowed on the bridge starting on May 12, 1925.[[220]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1925_e658-224)[[221]](#cite_note-p553994510-225)

As part of an experiment, starting in November 1946, the Manhattan-bound roadway carried Brooklyn-bound traffic during the evening rush hours. The experiment ended after two months due to complaints about congestion.[[222]](#cite_note-226)

### Mid- to late 20th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Mid- to late 20th century")]

#### Upgrades

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Upgrades")]

The first major upgrade to the Brooklyn Bridge commenced in 1948, when a contract to entirely reconstruct the approach ramps was awarded to [David B. Steinman](/wiki/David_B._Steinman "David B. Steinman").[[223]](#cite_note-nyt19480904-227)[[224]](#cite_note-228) The renovation was expected to double the capacity of the bridge's roadways to nearly 6,000 cars per hour,[[223]](#cite_note-nyt19480904-227) at a projected cost of $7 million.[[30]](#cite_note-nyt19500920-32) The renovation included the demolition of both the elevated and the trolley tracks on the roadways, the removal of trusses separating the inner elevated tracks from the existing vehicle lanes and the widening of each roadway from two to three lanes,[[30]](#cite_note-nyt19500920-32)[[31]](#cite_note-:2-33) as well as the construction of a new steel-and-concrete floor.[[225]](#cite_note-229) In addition, new ramps were added to Adams Street, [Cadman Plaza](/wiki/Cadman_Plaza "Cadman Plaza"), and the [Brooklyn Queens Expressway](/wiki/Brooklyn_Queens_Expressway "Brooklyn Queens Expressway") (BQE) on the Brooklyn side, and to Park Row on the Manhattan side.[[226]](#cite_note-230) The bridge was briefly closed to all traffic for the first time ever in January 1950,[[227]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1950_m819-231) and the trolley tracks closed that March to allow the widening work to occur.[[228]](#cite_note-Small_p._20-232)[[229]](#cite_note-nyt19500303-233) During the construction project, one roadway at a time was closed, allowing reduced traffic flows to cross the bridge in one direction only.[[230]](#cite_note-nydn19540502-234)

The widened south roadway was completed in May 1951,[[231]](#cite_note-235) followed by the north roadway in October 1953.[[232]](#cite_note-236) The restoration was finished in May 1954 with the completion of the reconstructed elevated promenade.[[233]](#cite_note-nyt19540504-237)[[230]](#cite_note-nydn19540502-234) While the rebuilding of the span was ongoing, a fallout shelter was constructed beneath the Manhattan approach in anticipation of the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"). The abandoned space in one of the masonry arches was stocked with emergency survival supplies for a potential nuclear attack by the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"); these supplies remained in place half a century later.[[234]](#cite_note-238) In addition, defensive barriers were added to the bridge as a safeguard against sabotage.[[235]](#cite_note-239)

[![Ramp from the Brooklyn Bridge to FDR Drive, completed circa 1969](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/BrooklynBridgeRampsMANH.jpg/250px-BrooklynBridgeRampsMANH.jpg)](/wiki/File:BrooklynBridgeRampsMANH.jpg)

Ramp from the Brooklyn Bridge to FDR Drive (pictured in 2008), completed c. 1969

Simultaneous with the rebuilding of the Brooklyn Bridge, a double-decked viaduct for the BQE was being built through an existing steel overpass of the bridge's Brooklyn approach ramp.[[236]](#cite_note-240) The segment of the BQE from Brooklyn Bridge south to [Atlantic Avenue](/wiki/Atlantic_Avenue_(New_York_City) "Atlantic Avenue (New York City)") opened in June 1954,[[237]](#cite_note-241) but the direct ramp from the northbound BQE to the Manhattan-bound Brooklyn Bridge did not open until 1959.[[238]](#cite_note-242) The city also widened the Adams Street approach in Brooklyn, between the bridge and [Fulton Street](/wiki/Fulton_Street_(Brooklyn) "Fulton Street (Brooklyn)"), from 60 to 160 feet (18 to 49 m) between 1954 and 1955.[[239]](#cite_note-243)[[240]](#cite_note-244) Subsequently, Boerum Place from Fulton Street south to Atlantic Avenue was also widened.[[241]](#cite_note-245) This required the demolition of the old Kings County courthouse.[[242]](#cite_note-246) The towers were cleaned in 1958[[243]](#cite_note-247) and the Brooklyn anchorage was repaired the next year.[[244]](#cite_note-248)

On the Manhattan side, the city approved a controversial rebuilding of the Manhattan entrance plaza in 1953. The project, which would add a grade-separated junction over Park Row, was hotly contested because it would require the demolition of 21 structures, including the old [New York World Building](/wiki/New_York_World_Building "New York World Building").[[245]](#cite_note-249) The reconstruction also necessitated the relocation of 410 families on Park Row.[[246]](#cite_note-250) In December 1956, the city started a two-year renovation of the plaza. This required the closure of one roadway at a time, as was done during the rebuilding of the bridge itself.[[247]](#cite_note-251) Work on redeveloping the area around the Manhattan approach started in the mid-1960s.[[248]](#cite_note-252) At the same time, plans were announced for direct ramps to the elevated [FDR Drive](/wiki/FDR_Drive "FDR Drive") to alleviate congestion at the approach.[[249]](#cite_note-253) The ramp from FDR Drive to the Brooklyn Bridge was opened in 1968,[[250]](#cite_note-254) followed by the ramp from the bridge to FDR Drive the next year.[[251]](#cite_note-255) A single ramp from the Manhattan-bound Brooklyn Bridge to northbound Park Row was constructed in 1970.[[252]](#cite_note-256) A repainting of the bridge was announced two years later in advance of its 90th anniversary.[[253]](#cite_note-257)

#### Deterioration and late-20th century repair

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Deterioration and late-20th century repair")]

[![A view of the Brooklyn Bridge in 1982, showing the steps that formerly led to the pedestrian promenade. A suspension tower is located in the background](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/View_looking_towards_Manhattan_of_steps_up_to_Promenade_with_Brooklyn_Tower_in_background._Jet_Lowe%2C_photographer%2C_1982_-_Brooklyn_Bridge%2C_Spanning_East_River_between_Park_Row%2C_HAER_NY%2C31-NEYO%2C90-68.tif/lossy-page1-250px-thumbnail.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:View_looking_towards_Manhattan_of_steps_up_to_Promenade_with_Brooklyn_Tower_in_background._Jet_Lowe,_photographer,_1982_-_Brooklyn_Bridge,_Spanning_East_River_between_Park_Row,_HAER_NY,31-NEYO,90-68.tif)

The Brooklyn Bridge formerly had steps up to the promenade from the Brooklyn approach (seen here in 1982).

The Brooklyn Bridge gradually deteriorated due to age and neglect. While it had 200 full-time dedicated maintenance workers before World War II, that number dropped to five by the late 20th century, and the city as a whole only had 160 bridge maintenance workers.[[254]](#cite_note-258) In 1974, heavy vehicles such as vans and buses were banned from the bridge to prevent further erosion of the concrete roadway.[[255]](#cite_note-259) A report in *The New York Times* four years later noted that the cables were visibly fraying and the pedestrian promenade had holes in it.[[256]](#cite_note-260) The city began planning to replace all the Brooklyn Bridge's cables at a cost of $115 million, as part of a larger project to renovate the Brooklyn, Manhattan, Williamsburg, and Queensboro bridges.[[257]](#cite_note-nyt19810630-261) By 1980, the Brooklyn Bridge was in such dire condition that it faced imminent closure. In some places, half of the strands in the cables were broken.[[258]](#cite_note-262)

In June 1981, two of the diagonal stay cables snapped,[[259]](#cite_note-263) killing a pedestrian.[[260]](#cite_note-nydn19810630-264)[[26]](#cite_note-nyt19860308-28) Subsequently, the anchorages were found to have developed rust,[[260]](#cite_note-nydn19810630-264) and an emergency cable repair was necessitated less than a month later after another cable developed slack.[[261]](#cite_note-265) Following the incident, the city accelerated the timetable of its proposed cable replacement,[[257]](#cite_note-nyt19810630-261) and it commenced a $153 million rehabilitation of the Brooklyn Bridge in advance of the 100th anniversary. As part of the project, the bridge's original suspender cables installed by J. Lloyd Haigh were replaced by [Bethlehem Steel](/wiki/Bethlehem_Steel "Bethlehem Steel") in 1986, marking the cables' first replacement since construction.[[26]](#cite_note-nyt19860308-28)[[52]](#cite_note-Morning_Call_m830-54) In addition, the staircase at Washington Street in Brooklyn was renovated,[[262]](#cite_note-266) the stairs from Tillary and Adams Streets were replaced with a ramp, and the short flights of steps from the promenade to each tower's balcony were removed.[[263]](#cite_note-267) In a smaller project, the bridge was floodlit at night starting in 1982 to highlight its architectural features.[[264]](#cite_note-268)

Additional problems persisted, and in 1993, high levels of [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead") were discovered near the bridge's towers.[[265]](#cite_note-269) Further emergency repairs were undertaken in mid-1999 after small concrete shards began falling from the bridge into the East River. The concrete deck had been installed during the 1950s renovations and had a lifespan of about 60 years.[[266]](#cite_note-270)[[267]](#cite_note-271) The Park Row exit from the bridge's westbound lanes was closed as a safety measure after the [September 11, 2001, attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") on the nearby [World Trade Center](/wiki/World_Trade_Center_(1973%E2%80%932001) "World Trade Center (1973–2001)"). That section of Park Row had been closed off since it ran right underneath [1 Police Plaza](/wiki/1_Police_Plaza "1 Police Plaza"), the headquarters of the [New York City Police Department](/wiki/New_York_City_Police_Department "New York City Police Department") (NYPD).[[268]](#cite_note-Buckley_2007-272) In early 2003, to save money on electricity, the NYCDOT turned off the bridge's "necklace lights" at night.[[269]](#cite_note-273) They were turned back on later that year after several private entities made donations to fund the lights.[[270]](#cite_note-274)

### 21st century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: 21st century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f2/Brooklyn_Bridge_-_New_York_City.jpg/330px-Brooklyn_Bridge_-_New_York_City.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Bridge_-_New_York_City.jpg)

In 2004, before renovation
