[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Brooklyn_Bridge-New_York.jpg/330px-Brooklyn_Bridge-New_York.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Bridge-New_York.jpg)

Renovation in progress

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Brooklyn_Bridge_September_2022_008.jpg/330px-Brooklyn_Bridge_September_2022_008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Bridge_September_2022_008.jpg)

In 2022, after renovation

After the 2007 collapse of the [I-35W bridge](/wiki/I-35W_Mississippi_River_bridge "I-35W Mississippi River bridge") in [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis"), public attention focused on the condition of bridges across the U.S. *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* reported that the Brooklyn Bridge approach ramps had received a "poor" rating during an inspection in 2007.[[271]](#cite_note-275) However, a NYCDOT spokesman said that the poor rating did not indicate a dangerous state but rather implied it required renovation.[[272]](#cite_note-276) In 2010, the NYCDOT began renovating the approaches and deck, as well as repainting the suspension span.[[273]](#cite_note-NYDN-Renovate-2010-277)[[274]](#cite_note-278) Work included widening two approach ramps from one to two lanes by re-striping a new prefabricated ramp; raising clearance over the eastbound BQE at York Street; seismic retrofitting; replacement of rusted railings and safety barriers; and road deck resurfacing.[[275]](#cite_note-279) The work necessitated detours for four years.[[276]](#cite_note-280) At the time, the project was scheduled to be completed in 2014;[[273]](#cite_note-NYDN-Renovate-2010-277) but completion was later delayed to 2015,[[277]](#cite_note-281) then again to 2017.[[278]](#cite_note-Sugar_2016-282) The project's cost also increased from $508 million in 2010[[273]](#cite_note-NYDN-Renovate-2010-277) to $811 million in 2016.[[278]](#cite_note-Sugar_2016-282)

In August 2016, the NYCDOT announced that it would conduct a seven-month, $370,000 study to verify if the bridge could support a heavier upper deck that consisted of an expanded bicycle and pedestrian path. By then, about 10,000 pedestrians and 3,500 cyclists used the pathway on an average weekday.[[279]](#cite_note-nyt20160809-283) Work on the pedestrian entrance on the Brooklyn side was underway by 2017.[[280]](#cite_note-284) The NYCDOT also indicated in 2016 that it planned to reinforce the Brooklyn Bridge's foundations to prevent it from sinking, as well as repair the masonry arches on the approach ramps, which had been damaged by [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") four years earlier.[[281]](#cite_note-285) In July 2018, the [New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission](/wiki/New_York_City_Landmarks_Preservation_Commission "New York City Landmarks Preservation Commission") approved a further renovation of the Brooklyn Bridge's suspension towers and approach ramps.[[282]](#cite_note-286) That December, the federal government gave the city $25 million in funding, which would pay for a $337 million rehabilitation of the bridge approaches and the suspension towers.[[283]](#cite_note-287) Work started in late 2019 and was scheduled to be completed in four years.[[284]](#cite_note-288) This restoration included removing bricks from the arches and putting fresh concrete behind them, using mortar from the same upstate quarries as the original mortar. The granite arches were also cleaned, revealing the original gray color of the stone, which had long been hidden by grime.[[285]](#cite_note-289) Additionally, 56 LED lamps were installed on the bridge at a cost of $2.4 million.[[62]](#cite_note-Simko-Bednarski_2024_a204-65)

In early 2020, [City Council](/wiki/New_York_City_Council "New York City Council") speaker [Corey Johnson](/wiki/Corey_Johnson_(politician) "Corey Johnson (politician)") and the nonprofit [Van Alen Institute](/wiki/Van_Alen_Institute "Van Alen Institute") hosted an international contest to solicit plans for the redesign of the bridge's walkway.[[286]](#cite_note-290)[[287]](#cite_note-291) Ultimately, in January 2021, the city decided to install a two-way [protected bike path](/wiki/Protected_bicycle_lanes "Protected bicycle lanes") on the Manhattan-bound roadway, replacing the leftmost vehicular lane. The bike lane would allow the existing promenade to be used exclusively by pedestrians.[[288]](#cite_note-292)[[289]](#cite_note-293) Work on the bike lane started in June 2021,[[290]](#cite_note-294)[[291]](#cite_note-295) and the new path was completed on September 14, 2021.[[292]](#cite_note-Offenhartz_2021-296) Despite the addition of the bike path, the bridge's walkway was still frequently overcrowded,[[293]](#cite_note-Budds_2023_z625-297) prompting the city to propose that street vendors be banned from the bridge and others citywide.[[294]](#cite_note-Frost_2023_a100-298) All vendors were banned from the bridge in January 2024,[[295]](#cite_note-ABC7_New_York_2024_l197-299) and the same month, the bridge's new LED lights were illuminated for the first time.[[62]](#cite_note-Simko-Bednarski_2024_a204-65) The [National Transportation Safety Board](/wiki/National_Transportation_Safety_Board "National Transportation Safety Board") recommended in early 2025 that the bridge undergo a structural vulnerability assessment, following the [Francis Scott Key Bridge collapse](/wiki/Francis_Scott_Key_Bridge_collapse "Francis Scott Key Bridge collapse") in Maryland the previous year.[[296]](#cite_note-300)

## Usage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Usage")]

[![A diagram of the pedestrian and former cycle path above and between the roadways, which is not to scale](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7e/Brooklyn_bridge_section.svg/250px-Brooklyn_bridge_section.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_bridge_section.svg)

Diagram of the pedestrian and former cycle path above and between the roadways (not to scale), before the new cycle path was opened in 2021

### Vehicular traffic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Vehicular traffic")]

Horse-drawn carriages have been allowed to use the Brooklyn Bridge's roadways since its opening. Originally, each of the two roadways carried two lanes of a different direction of traffic.[[6]](#cite_note-Barnes_p._28-6) The lanes were relatively narrow at only 8 feet (2.4 m) wide.[[233]](#cite_note-nyt19540504-237) In July 1922, motor vehicles were banned from the bridge;[[215]](#cite_note-nyt19220707-219) the ban lasted until May 1925.[[220]](#cite_note-The_New_York_Times_1925_e658-224)[[221]](#cite_note-p553994510-225)

After 1950, the main roadway carried six lanes of automobile traffic, three in each direction.[[229]](#cite_note-nyt19500303-233) It was then reduced to five lanes with the addition of a two-way bike lane on the Manhattan-bound side in 2021.[[297]](#cite_note-301)[[298]](#cite_note-302) Because of the roadway's posted height restriction of 11 ft (3.4 m) and weight restriction of 6,000 lb (2,700 kg), commercial vehicles and buses are prohibited from using the Brooklyn Bridge.[[299]](#cite_note-303) The weight restrictions prohibit heavy passenger vehicles such as pickup trucks and SUVs from using the bridge, though this is not often enforced in practice.[[300]](#cite_note-304)

On the Brooklyn side, vehicles can enter the bridge from Tillary/Adams Streets to the south, Sands/Pearl Streets to the west, and exit 28B of the eastbound [Brooklyn-Queens Expressway](/wiki/Brooklyn-Queens_Expressway "Brooklyn-Queens Expressway"). In Manhattan, cars can enter from both the northbound and southbound FDR Drive, as well as Park Row to the west, [Chambers](/wiki/Chambers_Street_(Manhattan) "Chambers Street (Manhattan)")/Centre Streets to the north, and Pearl Street to the south.[[301]](#cite_note-google-305) However, the exit from the bridge to northbound Park Row was closed after the September 11 attacks because of increased security concerns: that section of Park Row ran under One Police Plaza, the NYPD headquarters.[[268]](#cite_note-Buckley_2007-272)

#### Exit list

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Exit list")]

Vehicular access to the bridge is provided by a complex series of ramps on both sides of the bridge. There are two entrances to the bridge's [pedestrian promenade](#Walkway) on either side.[[301]](#cite_note-google-305) The current configuration, including the closed ramp from the bridge to the northbound Park Row, was constructed from the mid-1950s until the early 1970s.[[268]](#cite_note-Buckley_2007-272)
All exits are unnumbered.

### Rail traffic

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Rail traffic")]

Formerly, rail traffic operated on the Brooklyn Bridge as well. Cable cars and elevated railroads used the bridge until 1944, while trolleys ran until 1950.[[228]](#cite_note-Small_p._20-232)[[229]](#cite_note-nyt19500303-233)

#### Cable cars and elevated railroads

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Cable cars and elevated railroads")]

[Thomas A. Edison, Inc.](/wiki/Thomas_A._Edison,_Inc. "Thomas A. Edison, Inc."): "New Brooklyn to New York Via Brooklyn Bridge", 1899

The New York and Brooklyn Bridge Railway, a cable car service, began operating on September 25, 1883; it ran on the inner lanes of the bridge, between terminals at the Manhattan and Brooklyn ends.[[192]](#cite_note-nyt18830925-196)[[193]](#cite_note-Small_p._5-197) Since Washington Roebling believed that [steam locomotives](/wiki/Steam_locomotive "Steam locomotive") would put excessive loads upon the structure of the Brooklyn Bridge, the cable car line was designed as a steam/cable-hauled hybrid. They were powered from a generating station under the Brooklyn approach. The cable cars could not only regulate their speed on the 3+3⁄4% upward and downward approaches, but also maintain a constant interval between each other. There were 24 cable cars in total.[[302]](#cite_note-306)

Initially, the service ran with single-car trains, but patronage soon grew so much that by October 1883, two-car trains were in use. The line carried three million people in the first six months, nine million in 1884, and nearly 20 million in 1885 following the opening of the [Brooklyn Union Elevated Railroad](/wiki/Brooklyn_Union_Elevated_Railroad "Brooklyn Union Elevated Railroad"). Accordingly, the track layout was rearranged and more trains were ordered.[[182]](#cite_note-bc18830531-186)[[303]](#cite_note-307) At the same time, there were highly controversial plans to extend the elevated railroads onto the Brooklyn Bridge, under the pretext of extending the bridge itself.[[304]](#cite_note-308) After disputes, the trustees agreed to build two elevated routes to the bridge on the Brooklyn side.[[305]](#cite_note-309) Patronage continued to increase, and in 1888, the tracks were lengthened and even more cars were constructed to allow for four-car cable car trains.[[197]](#cite_note-Small_pp._10-11-201) Electric wires for the trolleys were added by 1895, allowing for the potential future decommissioning of the steam/cable system.[[306]](#cite_note-310) The terminals were rebuilt once more in July 1895, and, following the implementation of new electric cars in late 1896, the steam engines were dismantled and sold.[[307]](#cite_note-311)

Following the [unification](/wiki/City_of_Greater_New_York#Consolidation "City of Greater New York") of the cities of New York and Brooklyn in 1898, the New York and Brooklyn Bridge Railway ceased to be a separate entity that June and the Brooklyn Rapid Transit Company (BRT) assumed control of the line. The BRT started running through-services of elevated trains, which ran from [Park Row Terminal](/wiki/Park_Row_Terminal "Park Row Terminal") in Manhattan to points in Brooklyn via the [Sands Street station](/wiki/Sands_Street_station "Sands Street station") on the Brooklyn side. Before reaching Sands Street (at [Tillary Street](/wiki/Clark%E2%80%93Tillary_Streets_station "Clark–Tillary Streets station") for [Fulton Street Line](/wiki/Fulton_Street_Line_(elevated) "Fulton Street Line (elevated)") trains, and at [Bridge Street](/wiki/Bridge%E2%80%93Jay_Streets_station "Bridge–Jay Streets station") for [Fifth Avenue Line](/wiki/Fifth_Avenue_Line_(Brooklyn_elevated) "Fifth Avenue Line (Brooklyn elevated)") and [Myrtle Avenue Line](/wiki/BMT_Myrtle_Avenue_Line "BMT Myrtle Avenue Line") trains), elevated trains bound for Manhattan were uncoupled from their steam locomotives. The elevated trains were then coupled to the cable cars, which would pull the passenger carriages across the bridge.[[308]](#cite_note-312)

The BRT did not run any elevated train through services from 1899 to 1901. Due to increased patronage after the opening of the [Interborough Rapid Transit Company](/wiki/Interborough_Rapid_Transit_Company "Interborough Rapid Transit Company") (IRT)'s [first subway line](/wiki/Early_history_of_the_IRT_subway "Early history of the IRT subway"), the Park Row station was rebuilt in 1906.[[309]](#cite_note-313) In the early 20th century, there were plans for Brooklyn Bridge elevated trains to run underground to the BRT's proposed [Chambers Street station](/wiki/Chambers_Street_station_(BMT_Nassau_Street_Line) "Chambers Street station (BMT Nassau Street Line)") in Manhattan,[[310]](#cite_note-314) though the connection was never opened.[[311]](#cite_note-315)[[312]](#cite_note-Small_p._18-316) The overpass across [William Street](/wiki/William_Street_(Manhattan) "William Street (Manhattan)") was closed in 1913 to make way for the proposed connection. In 1929, the overpass was reopened after it became clear that the connection would not be built.[[313]](#cite_note-317)

After the IRT's [Joralemon Street Tunnel](/wiki/Joralemon_Street_Tunnel "Joralemon Street Tunnel") and the [Williamsburg Bridge tracks](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge#Rail_tracks "Williamsburg Bridge") opened in 1908, the Brooklyn Bridge no longer held a monopoly on rail service between Manhattan and Brooklyn, and cable service ceased.[[312]](#cite_note-Small_p._18-316) New subway lines from the IRT and from the BRT's successor [Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation](/wiki/Brooklyn%E2%80%93Manhattan_Transit_Corporation "Brooklyn–Manhattan Transit Corporation") (BMT), built in the 1910s and 1920s, posed significant competition to the Brooklyn Bridge rail services. With the opening of the [Independent Subway System](/wiki/Independent_Subway_System "Independent Subway System") in 1932 and the subsequent unification of all three companies into a single entity in 1940, the elevated services started to decline, and the Park Row and Sands Street stations were greatly reduced in size. The Fifth Avenue and Fulton Street services across the Brooklyn Bridge were discontinued in 1940 and 1941 respectively, and the elevated tracks were abandoned permanently with the withdrawal of Myrtle Avenue services in 1944.[[228]](#cite_note-Small_p._20-232)

#### Trolleys

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Trolleys")]

See also: [Brooklyn Bridge trolleys](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge_trolleys "Brooklyn Bridge trolleys")

A plan for trolley service across the Brooklyn Bridge was presented in 1895.[[314]](#cite_note-318) Two years later, the Brooklyn Bridge trustees agreed to a plan where trolleys could run across the bridge under ten-year contracts.[[315]](#cite_note-319) Trolley service, which began in 1898, ran on what are now the two middle lanes of each roadway (shared with other traffic).[[200]](#cite_note-Small_p._14-204)[[201]](#cite_note-nyt18980217-205) Brooklyn Bridge Local trolley service between the two ends of the bridge was introduced in January 1908.[[316]](#cite_note-Cudahy-320)[[317]](#cite_note-pamphlet-321) The cable cars were discontinued immediately afterward, and the shuttle was also eliminated.[[318]](#cite_note-322) After cable service was withdrawn, the trolley tracks on the Brooklyn side were rebuilt to alleviate congestion.[[312]](#cite_note-Small_p._18-316) Trolley service on the middle lanes continued until the elevated lines stopped using the bridge in 1944, when they moved to the protected center tracks. On March 5, 1950, the streetcars also stopped running, and the bridge was redesigned exclusively for automobile traffic.[[228]](#cite_note-Small_p._20-232)[[229]](#cite_note-nyt19500303-233)

### Walkway

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Walkway")]

[![The Brooklyn Bridge's elevated pedestrian promenade](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/BrooklynBridgeNoPeople.png/250px-BrooklynBridgeNoPeople.png)](/wiki/File:BrooklynBridgeNoPeople.png)

The Brooklyn Bridge's elevated pedestrian promenade, near one of the "pinch points" where the cables descend below the height of the girders

The Brooklyn Bridge has an elevated promenade open to pedestrians in the center of the bridge, located 18 feet (5.5 m) above the automobile lanes.[[33]](#cite_note-nyt19850816-35) The promenade is usually located 4 feet (1.2 m) below the height of the girders, except at the approach ramps leading to each tower's balcony.[[34]](#cite_note-BBPr_p._26-27-36) The path is generally 10 to 17 feet (3.0 to 5.2 m) wide,[[36]](#cite_note-amny20160809-38)[[34]](#cite_note-BBPr_p._26-27-36) though this is constrained by obstacles such as protruding cables, benches, and stairways, which create "pinch points" at certain locations.[[319]](#cite_note-323) The path narrows to 10 feet (3.0 m) at the locations where the main cables descend to the level of the promenade. Further exacerbating the situation, these "pinch points" are some of the most popular places to take pictures.[[320]](#cite_note-324) As a result, in 2016, the NYCDOT announced that it planned to double the promenade's width.[[36]](#cite_note-amny20160809-38)[[279]](#cite_note-nyt20160809-283)

A center line was painted to separate cyclists from pedestrians in 1971, creating one of the city's first dedicated bike lanes.[[321]](#cite_note-325) Initially, the northern side of the promenade was used by pedestrians and the southern side by cyclists. In 2000, these were swapped, with cyclists taking the northern side and pedestrians taking the southern side.[[322]](#cite_note-326) On September 14, 2021, the NYCDOT closed off the left-side car lane on the Manhattan-bound side with protective barriers and fencing to create a new bike path, and cyclists were banned from the upper pedestrian lane.[[292]](#cite_note-Offenhartz_2021-296)

Pedestrian access to the bridge from the Brooklyn side is from either the median of Adams Street at its intersection with Tillary Street or a staircase near Prospect Street between Cadman Plaza East and West. In Manhattan, the pedestrian walkway is accessible from crosswalks at the intersection of the bridge and Centre Street, or through a staircase leading to Park Row.[[301]](#cite_note-google-305)[[323]](#cite_note-327)

#### Emergency use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Emergency use")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/BrooklynBridgeWalkwayCrowd.jpg/250px-BrooklynBridgeWalkwayCrowd.jpg)](/wiki/File:BrooklynBridgeWalkwayCrowd.jpg)

The elevated pedestrian promenade crowded with people, in the evening, during the summer season.

While the bridge has always permitted the passage of pedestrians, the promenade facilitates movement when other means of crossing the East River have become unavailable. During transit strikes by the [Transport Workers Union](/wiki/Transport_Workers_Union_of_America "Transport Workers Union of America") in [1980](/wiki/1980_New_York_City_transit_strike "1980 New York City transit strike") and [2005](/wiki/2005_New_York_City_transit_strike "2005 New York City transit strike"), people commuting to work used the bridge; they were joined by Mayors [Ed Koch](/wiki/Ed_Koch "Ed Koch") and [Michael Bloomberg](/wiki/Michael_Bloomberg "Michael Bloomberg"), who crossed as a gesture to the affected public.[[324]](#cite_note-328)[[325]](#cite_note-329) Pedestrians also walked across the bridge as an alternative to suspended subway services following the [1965](/wiki/Northeast_Blackout_of_1965 "Northeast Blackout of 1965"),[[326]](#cite_note-330) [1977](/wiki/New_York_City_blackout_of_1977 "New York City blackout of 1977"),[[327]](#cite_note-331) and [2003](/wiki/2003_North_America_blackout "2003 North America blackout") [blackouts](/wiki/Power_outage "Power outage"),[[328]](#cite_note-332) and after the September 11 attacks.[[329]](#cite_note-333)

During the 2003 blackouts, many crossing the bridge reported a swaying motion.[[330]](#cite_note-voice-334) The higher-than-usual pedestrian load caused this swaying, which was amplified by the tendency of pedestrians to synchronize their footfalls with a sway.[[331]](#cite_note-335) Several engineers expressed concern about how this would affect the bridge, although others noted that it did withstand the event and that the redundancies in its design—the inclusion of the three support systems (suspension system, diagonal stay system, and stiffening [truss](/wiki/Truss "Truss"))—make it "probably the best secured bridge against such movements going out of control."[[330]](#cite_note-voice-334) In designing the bridge, John Roebling had stated that the bridge would sag but not fall, even if one of these structural systems were to fail altogether.[[79]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._32-33-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3b/Brooklyn_Bridge_panorama.jpg/1280px-Brooklyn_Bridge_panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Bridge_panorama.jpg "Panorama of Brooklyn Bridge, with the Manhattan Bridge behind it, and the Williamsburg Bridge visible farther in the background")

Panorama of Brooklyn Bridge, with the [Manhattan Bridge](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge") behind it, and the [Williamsburg Bridge](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge") visible farther in the background

## Tolls

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Tolls")]

Further information: [Congestion pricing in New York City § Tolls](/wiki/Congestion_pricing_in_New_York_City#Tolls "Congestion pricing in New York City")

The Brooklyn Bridge was initially a [toll bridge](/wiki/Toll_bridge "Toll bridge"). Though carriages and cable-car customers had paid tolls ever since the bridge's opening, pedestrians were spared from the tolls originally.[[169]](#cite_note-nyt18830313-173) By the first decade of the 20th century, pedestrians were also paying tolls.[[332]](#cite_note-336) Tolls on all four bridges across the East River—the Brooklyn Bridge, as well as the [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan_Bridge "Manhattan Bridge"), [Williamsburg](/wiki/Williamsburg_Bridge "Williamsburg Bridge"), and [Queensboro](/wiki/Queensboro_Bridge "Queensboro Bridge") bridges to the north—were abolished in July 1911 as part of a populist policy initiative headed by New York City mayor [William Jay Gaynor](/wiki/William_Jay_Gaynor "William Jay Gaynor").[[212]](#cite_note-nyt19110719-216)[[213]](#cite_note-n33421526-217)

In 1970, the federal government enacted the [Clean Air Act](/wiki/Clean_Air_Act_(United_States) "Clean Air Act (United States)"), a series of federal air pollution regulations.[[333]](#cite_note-337) As part of a plan by mayor [John Lindsay](/wiki/John_Lindsay "John Lindsay") and the federal [Environmental Protection Agency](/wiki/United_States_Environmental_Protection_Agency "United States Environmental Protection Agency"),[[334]](#cite_note-NYT_1977_mayor_yields-338) the city government considered implementing tolls on the four free East River bridges, including the Brooklyn Bridge, in the early 1970s.[[335]](#cite_note-339)[[336]](#cite_note-340) The plan would have raised money for New York City's transit system[[337]](#cite_note-341) and allowed the city to meet the Clean Air Act.[[334]](#cite_note-NYT_1977_mayor_yields-338) [Abraham Beame](/wiki/Abraham_Beame "Abraham Beame"), who became mayor in 1974, refused to implement the tolls,[[338]](#cite_note-342) and the [United States Congress](/wiki/United_States_Congress "United States Congress") subsequently moved to forbid tolls on the free East River bridges.[[334]](#cite_note-NYT_1977_mayor_yields-338) The [United States Department of Transportation](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Transportation "United States Department of Transportation") determined that several of these bridges were built partially with federal funds and, under federal law, could not be tolled.[[339]](#cite_note-343)

A plan for [congestion pricing in New York City](/wiki/Congestion_pricing_in_New_York_City "Congestion pricing in New York City") was approved in mid-2023,[[340]](#cite_note-344) allowing the [Metropolitan Transportation Authority](/wiki/Metropolitan_Transportation_Authority "Metropolitan Transportation Authority") to toll drivers who enter Manhattan south of [60th Street](/wiki/60th_Street_(Manhattan) "60th Street (Manhattan)").[[341]](#cite_note-Nessen_2024_t852-345) Congestion pricing was implemented in January 2025.[[342]](#cite_note-Anderson_o055-346) Most traffic between the Brooklyn Bridge and FDR Drive is exempt from the toll, but all other Manhattan-bound drivers pay a toll, which varies based on the time of day. Although no toll is charged upon exiting the congestion zone, Brooklyn-bound drivers must pay a toll to access streets leading to the bridge, unless they use the FDR Drive.[[341]](#cite_note-Nessen_2024_t852-345)[[e]](#cite_note-347)

## Notable events

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Notable events")]

### Stunts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Stunts")]

[![A sketch depicting Robert E. Odlum jumping from the bridge](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Odlum_jumps.jpg/250px-Odlum_jumps.jpg)](/wiki/File:Odlum_jumps.jpg)

[Robert Emmet Odlum](/wiki/Robert_Emmet_Odlum "Robert Emmet Odlum") jumping from the bridge on May 19, 1885

There have been several notable jumpers from the Brooklyn Bridge. The first person was [Robert Emmet Odlum](/wiki/Robert_Emmet_Odlum "Robert Emmet Odlum"), brother of women's rights activist [Charlotte Odlum Smith](/wiki/Charlotte_Odlum_Smith "Charlotte Odlum Smith"), on May 19, 1885.[[343]](#cite_note-348)[[344]](#cite_note-349) He struck the water at an angle and died shortly afterwards from internal injuries.[[345]](#cite_note-350) [Steve Brodie](/wiki/Steve_Brodie_(bridge_jumper) "Steve Brodie (bridge jumper)") supposedly dropped from underneath the bridge in July 1886 and was briefly arrested for it, though there is some doubt about whether he actually jumped.[[346]](#cite_note-351)[[196]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._546-547-200) [Larry Donovan](/wiki/Larry_Donovan_(bridge_jumper) "Larry Donovan (bridge jumper)") made a slightly higher jump from the railing a month afterward.[[196]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._546-547-200) The first known person to jump from the bridge with the intention of suicide was Francis McCarey in 1892.[[196]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._546-547-200) A lesser known early jumper was James Duffy of [County Cavan, Ireland](/wiki/County_Cavan,_Ireland "County Cavan, Ireland"), who on April 15, 1895, asked several men to watch him jump from the bridge. Duffy jumped and was not seen again.[[347]](#cite_note-352) Additionally, the cartoonist [Otto Eppers](/wiki/Otto_Eppers "Otto Eppers") jumped and survived in 1910, and was then tried and acquitted for attempted suicide.[[348]](#cite_note-353) The Brooklyn Bridge has since developed a reputation as a [suicide bridge](/wiki/Suicide_bridge "Suicide bridge") due to the number of jumpers who do so intending to kill themselves, though exact statistics are difficult to find.[[349]](#cite_note-354)

Other notable feats have taken place on or near the bridge. In 1919, [Giorgio Pessi](/wiki/Giorgio_Pessi "Giorgio Pessi") piloted what was then one of the world's largest airplanes, the [Caproni Ca.5](/wiki/Caproni_Ca.5 "Caproni Ca.5"), under the bridge.[[350]](#cite_note-355) In 1993, bridge jumper Thierry Devaux illegally performed eight acrobatic [bungee jumps](/wiki/Bungee_jumping "Bungee jumping") above the East River close to the Brooklyn tower.[[351]](#cite_note-356)

### Crimes and terrorism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Crimes and terrorism")]

[![NYPD officers take part in a training exercise on the Brooklyn Bridge by climbing a large cable leading to a pillar. Under the supervision of a helicopter.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/69/NYPD_training_exercise_on_Brooklyn_Bridge.jpg/250px-NYPD_training_exercise_on_Brooklyn_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:NYPD_training_exercise_on_Brooklyn_Bridge.jpg)

A police training exercise on the bridge, 2024

[On March 1, 1994](/wiki/1994_Brooklyn_Bridge_shooting "1994 Brooklyn Bridge shooting"), Lebanese-born Rashid Baz opened fire on a van carrying members of the [Chabad-Lubavitch](/wiki/Chabad-Lubavitch "Chabad-Lubavitch") [Orthodox Jewish](/wiki/Orthodox_Judaism "Orthodox Judaism") Movement, striking 16-year-old student Ari Halberstam and three others traveling on the bridge.[[352]](#cite_note-357) Halberstam died five days later from his wounds, and Baz was later convicted of murder. He was apparently acting out of revenge for the [Hebron massacre](/wiki/Cave_of_the_Patriarchs_massacre "Cave of the Patriarchs massacre") of Palestinian Muslims a few days prior to the incident.[[353]](#cite_note-358) After initially classifying the killing as one committed out of [road rage](/wiki/Road_rage_(phenomenon) "Road rage (phenomenon)"), the [Justice Department](/wiki/United_States_Department_of_Justice "United States Department of Justice") reclassified the case in 2000 as a terrorist attack.[[354]](#cite_note-359) The entrance ramp to the bridge on the Manhattan side was dedicated as the Ari Halberstam Memorial Ramp in 1995.[[355]](#cite_note-360)[[356]](#cite_note-361)

Several potential attacks or disasters have also been averted. In 1979, police disarmed a stick of dynamite placed under the Brooklyn approach,[[357]](#cite_note-362) and an artist in Manhattan was arrested that year after another bombing attempt.[[358]](#cite_note-363) In 2003, truck driver [Iyman Faris](/wiki/Iyman_Faris "Iyman Faris") was sentenced to about 20 years in prison for providing material support to [Al-Qaeda](/wiki/Al-Qaeda "Al-Qaeda"), after an earlier plot to destroy the bridge by cutting through its support wires with [blowtorches](/wiki/Blowtorch "Blowtorch") was thwarted.[[359]](#cite_note-364)

### Arrests

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Arrests")]

At 9:00 a.m. on May 19, 1977, artist Jack Bashkow climbed one of the towers for *Bridging*, a "media sculpture" by the performance group Art Corporation of America Inc. Seven artists climbed the largest bridges connected to Manhattan "to replace violence and fear in mass media for one day". When each of the artists had reached the tops of the bridges, they ignited bright-yellow [flares](/wiki/Flare "Flare") at the same moment, resulting in rush hour traffic disruption, media attention, and the arrest of the climbers, though the charges were later dropped. Called "the first social-sculpture to use mass-media as art" by conceptual artist [Joseph Beuys](/wiki/Joseph_Beuys "Joseph Beuys"),[[360]](#cite_note-365) the event was on the cover of the *[New York Post](/wiki/New_York_Post "New York Post")*, received international attention, and received [ABC Eyewitness News](/wiki/ABC_News_(United_States) "ABC News (United States)")' *1977 Best News of the Year* award.[[361]](#cite_note-366) [John Halpern](/wiki/John_Halpern_(artist) "John Halpern (artist)") documented the incident in the film *Bridging, 1977*. Halpern attempted another "bridging" "social sculpture" in 1979, when he planted a radio receiver, gunpowder and fireworks in a bucket atop one of the towers.[[362]](#cite_note-367) The piece was later discovered by police, leading to his arrest for possessing a bomb.[[363]](#cite_note-368)

On October 1, 2011, more than 700 protesters with the [Occupy Wall Street](/wiki/Occupy_Wall_Street "Occupy Wall Street") movement were arrested while attempting to march across the bridge on the roadway.[[364]](#cite_note-369) Protesters disputed the police account of the events and claimed that the arrests were the result of being trapped on the bridge by the NYPD.[[365]](#cite_note-370) The majority of the arrests were subsequently dismissed.[[366]](#cite_note-371)

On July 22, 2014, the two [American flags](/wiki/Flag_of_the_United_States "Flag of the United States") on the flagpoles atop each tower were found to have been replaced by bleached-white American flags.[[367]](#cite_note-372)[[368]](#cite_note-373) Initially, [cannabis](/wiki/Cannabis_(drug) "Cannabis (drug)") activism was suspected as a motive,[[369]](#cite_note-374)[[370]](#cite_note-375) but on August 12, 2014, two Berlin artists claimed responsibility for hoisting the two white flags, having switched out the original flags with their replicas. The artists said that the flags were meant to celebrate "the beauty of public space" and the anniversary of the death of German-born John Roebling, and they denied that it was an "anti-American statement".[[371]](#cite_note-376)[[372]](#cite_note-377)[[373]](#cite_note-378)

### Anniversary celebrations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Anniversary celebrations")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/52/Brooklyn_Bridge_seen_from_One_World_Trade_Center_Skypod.jpg/250px-Brooklyn_Bridge_seen_from_One_World_Trade_Center_Skypod.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklyn_Bridge_seen_from_One_World_Trade_Center_Skypod.jpg)

Brooklyn Bridge seen from One World Trade Center Skypod

The 50th-anniversary celebrations on May 24, 1933, included a ceremony featuring an airplane show, ships, and fireworks,[[374]](#cite_note-379) as well as a banquet.[[375]](#cite_note-380) During the centennial celebrations on May 24, 1983, a flotilla of ships visited the harbor, officials held parades,[[376]](#cite_note-381)[[377]](#cite_note-nydn19830525-382) and [Grucci Fireworks](/wiki/Grucci_Fireworks "Grucci Fireworks") held a fireworks display that evening.[[378]](#cite_note-383)[[377]](#cite_note-nydn19830525-382) For the centennial, the [Brooklyn Museum](/wiki/Brooklyn_Museum "Brooklyn Museum") exhibited a selection of the original drawings made for the bridge's construction, including those by Washington Roebling.[[379]](#cite_note-384) Media coverage of the centennial was declared "the public relations triumph of 1983" by *[Inc.](/wiki/Inc._(magazine) "Inc. (magazine)")*[[380]](#cite_note-385)

The 125th anniversary of the bridge's opening was celebrated by a five-day event on May 22–26, 2008, which included a live performance by the [Brooklyn Philharmonic](/wiki/Brooklyn_Philharmonic "Brooklyn Philharmonic"), a special lighting of the bridge's towers, and a fireworks display.[[381]](#cite_note-386) Other events included a film series, historical walking tours, information tents, a series of lectures and readings, a bicycle tour of Brooklyn, a miniature golf course featuring Brooklyn icons, and other musical and dance performances.[[382]](#cite_note-387) Just before the anniversary celebrations, artist [Paul St George](/wiki/Paul_St_George "Paul St George") installed the [Telectroscope](/wiki/Telectroscope "Telectroscope"), a video link on the Brooklyn side of the bridge that connected to a matching device on London's [Tower Bridge](/wiki/Tower_Bridge "Tower Bridge").[[383]](#cite_note-388) A renovated pedestrian connection to [Dumbo, Brooklyn](/wiki/Dumbo,_Brooklyn "Dumbo, Brooklyn"), was also reopened before the anniversary celebrations.[[384]](#cite_note-389)

### Collisions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Collisions")]

See also: [Cuauhtémoc Brooklyn Bridge collision](/wiki/Cuauht%C3%A9moc_Brooklyn_Bridge_collision "Cuauhtémoc Brooklyn Bridge collision")

Over the years, numerous vessels have struck the bridge.[[22]](#cite_note-nyt-2025-05-18-24)[[385]](#cite_note-Danner_u188-390) In 1921, the steel mast of the schooner *[Edward J. Lawrence](/wiki/Edward_J._Lawrence "Edward J. Lawrence")* was bent while being towed under the bridge deck.[[386]](#cite_note-391) and in 1935, three of the four steel masts on the freighter *Tirpitz* hit the bridge deck.[[387]](#cite_note-392) Additionally, the radar of the freighter *Hai Soo* was destroyed in 1986 when it struck a net below the Brooklyn Bridge's deck.[[388]](#cite_note-393)

The masts of the [Mexican Navy](/wiki/Mexican_Navy "Mexican Navy") training ship [ARM *Cuauhtémoc*](/wiki/ARM_Cuauht%C3%A9moc_(BE01) "ARM Cuauhtémoc (BE01)") [smashed into the bridge deck on May 17, 2025](/wiki/Cuauht%C3%A9moc_Brooklyn_Bridge_collision "Cuauhtémoc Brooklyn Bridge collision"), with approximately 200 people on the ship.[[389]](#cite_note-Collision_2025-05-17-394)[[390]](#cite_note-AP-2025-05-17-395) The ship masts were at least 20 feet (6.1 m) higher than the Brooklyn Bridge's navigational clearance of 127 feet (39 m).[[390]](#cite_note-AP-2025-05-17-395)[[22]](#cite_note-nyt-2025-05-18-24) Two people were killed and 19 injured, but the structure of the bridge was undamaged.[[22]](#cite_note-nyt-2025-05-18-24)[[389]](#cite_note-Collision_2025-05-17-394)

## Impact

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Impact")]

At the time of construction, contemporaries marveled at what technology was capable of, and the bridge became a symbol of the era's optimism. [John Perry Barlow](/wiki/John_Perry_Barlow "John Perry Barlow") wrote in the late 20th century of the "literal and genuinely religious leap of faith" embodied in the bridge's construction, saying that the "Brooklyn Bridge required of its builders faith in their ability to control technology".[[391]](#cite_note-396)

### Historical designations and plaques

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Historical designations and plaques")]

Brooklyn Bridge plaques

[![A plaque at the Manhattan tower, which mentions the bridge's dedication and renovation](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Brooklynbridge_tablet_200907.jpg/250px-Brooklynbridge_tablet_200907.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brooklynbridge_tablet_200907.jpg)

Dedication and renovation plaque, at Manhattan tower

[![A plaque that certifies the Brooklyn Bridge as a New York City designated landmark](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/New_York_City_03.jpg/250px-New_York_City_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:New_York_City_03.jpg)

New York City designated landmark plaque

The Brooklyn Bridge has been listed as a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark") since January 29, 1964,[[14]](#cite_note-nhlsum-15)[[392]](#cite_note-397)[[393]](#cite_note-398) and was subsequently added to the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") on October 15, 1966.[[13]](#cite_note-nris-14) The bridge has also been a [New York City designated landmark](/wiki/New_York_City_designated_landmark "New York City designated landmark") since August 24, 1967,[[2]](#cite_note-NYCL-2) and was designated a [National Historic Civil Engineering Landmark](/wiki/List_of_historic_civil_engineering_landmarks "List of historic civil engineering landmarks") in 1972.[[394]](#cite_note-399) In addition, it was placed on [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s list of tentative [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site") in 2017.[[395]](#cite_note-400)

A bronze plaque is attached to the Manhattan anchorage, which was constructed on the site of the [Samuel Osgood House](/wiki/Samuel_Osgood_House "Samuel Osgood House") at 1 Cherry Street in Manhattan. Named after [Samuel Osgood](/wiki/Samuel_Osgood "Samuel Osgood"), a Massachusetts politician and lawyer, it was built in 1770 and served as the first U.S. presidential mansion.[[396]](#cite_note-401) The Osgood House was demolished in 1856.[[397]](#cite_note-402)

Another plaque on the Manhattan side of the pedestrian promenade, installed by the city in 1975, indicates the bridge's status as a city landmark.[[398]](#cite_note-403)[[399]](#cite_note-404)

### Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Culture")]

The Brooklyn Bridge has had an impact on idiomatic [American English](/wiki/American_English "American English"). For example, references to "selling the Brooklyn Bridge" are frequent in American culture, sometimes presented as a historical reality but more often as an expression meaning an idea that strains credulity. [George C. Parker](/wiki/George_C._Parker "George C. Parker") and [William McCloundy](/wiki/William_McCloundy "William McCloundy") were two early 20th-century con men who may have perpetrated this scam successfully, particularly on new immigrants,[[400]](#cite_note-405) although the author of *The Brooklyn Bridge: A Cultural History* wrote, "No evidence exists that the bridge has ever been sold to a 'gullible outlander'".[[401]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHaw2005145-406)

To highlight the Brooklyn Bridge's cultural status, the city proposed building a Brooklyn Bridge museum near the bridge's Brooklyn end in the 1970s.[[402]](#cite_note-407) Though the museum was ultimately not constructed, as many as [10,000 drawings and documents](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge_architectural_drawings "Brooklyn Bridge architectural drawings") relating to it were found in a carpenter shop in Williamsburg in 1976.[[403]](#cite_note-nyt19760528-408) These documents were given to the [New York City Municipal Archives](/wiki/New_York_City_Municipal_Archives "New York City Municipal Archives"), where they are normally located,[[404]](#cite_note-409) though a selection of them were displayed at the [Whitney Museum of American Art](/wiki/Whitney_Museum_of_American_Art_(original_building) "Whitney Museum of American Art (original building)") when they were discovered.[[403]](#cite_note-nyt19760528-408)

#### Love locks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Love locks")]

[!["Love locks" on the Brooklyn Bridge. Couples inscribe a date and their initials onto a lock, attach it to the bridge, and throw the key into the water as a sign of their love.](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Love_locks_of_the_Brooklyn_Bridge.jpg/250px-Love_locks_of_the_Brooklyn_Bridge.jpg)](/wiki/File:Love_locks_of_the_Brooklyn_Bridge.jpg)

Love locks on the Brooklyn Bridge

As a tourist attraction, the Brooklyn Bridge is a popular site for clusters of [love locks](/wiki/Love_lock "Love lock"), wherein a couple inscribes a date and their initials onto a lock, attach it to the bridge, and throw the key into the water as a sign of their love. The practice is illegal in New York City and the NYPD can give violators a $100 fine. NYCDOT workers periodically remove the love locks from the bridge at a cost of $100,000 per year.[[405]](#cite_note-410)[[406]](#cite_note-411)[[407]](#cite_note-412) There have been private efforts to remove the locks as well; for example, one man led a group of lock-breakers to remove some locks in February 2013,[[408]](#cite_note-413) and a local woman periodically removed locks and garbage from the bridge in early 2026.[[409]](#cite_note-414)[[410]](#cite_note-415)

### Media

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Brooklyn_Bridge&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Media")]

The bridge is often featured in [wide shots](/wiki/Wide_shot "Wide shot") of the [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") skyline in television and film and has been depicted in numerous works of art.[[411]](#cite_note-416) Fictional works have used the Brooklyn Bridge as a setting; for instance, the dedication of a portion of the bridge, and the bridge itself, were key components in the 2001 film *[Kate & Leopold](/wiki/Kate_%26_Leopold "Kate & Leopold")*.[[412]](#cite_note-417) Furthermore, the Brooklyn Bridge has also served as an icon of America, with mentions in numerous songs, books, and poems.[[413]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._548-549-418) Among the most notable of these works is that of American [Modernist](/wiki/Modernist_literature "Modernist literature") poet [Hart Crane](/wiki/Hart_Crane "Hart Crane"), who used the Brooklyn Bridge as a central metaphor and organizing structure for his second book of poetry, *[The Bridge](/wiki/The_Bridge_(long_poem) "The Bridge (long poem)")* (1930).[[413]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._548-549-418)[[414]](#cite_note-419)

The Brooklyn Bridge has also been lauded for its architecture. One of the first positive reviews was "The Bridge As A Monument", a *[Harper's Weekly](/wiki/Harper%27s_Weekly "Harper's Weekly")* piece written by architecture critic [Montgomery Schuyler](/wiki/Montgomery_Schuyler "Montgomery Schuyler") and published a week after the bridge's opening. In the piece, Schuyler wrote: "It so happens that the work which is likely to be our most durable monument, and to convey some knowledge of us to the most remote posterity, is a work of bare utility; not a shrine, not a fortress, not a palace, but a bridge."[[415]](#cite_note-420) Architecture critic [Lewis Mumford](/wiki/Lewis_Mumford "Lewis Mumford") cited the piece as the impetus for serious architectural criticism in the U.S.[[413]](#cite_note-McCullough_pp._548-549-418) He wrote that in the 1920s the bridge was a source of "joy and inspiration" in his childhood,[[416]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._550-421) and that it was a profound influence in his adolescence.[[417]](#cite_note-422) Later critics regarded the Brooklyn Bridge as a work of art, as opposed to an engineering feat or a means of transport.[[416]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._550-421) Conversely, [Henry James](/wiki/Henry_James "Henry James"), writing in the early 20th century, cited the bridge as an ominous symbol of the city's transformation into a "steel-souled machine room".[[416]](#cite_note-McCullough_p._550-421)[[418]](#cite_note-423)

The construction of the Brooklyn Bridge is detailed in numerous media sources, including [David McCullough](/wiki/David_McCullough "David McCullough")'s 1972 book *[The Great Bridge](/wiki/The_Great_Bridge_(book) "The Great Bridge (book)")*[[419]](#cite_note-424) and [Ken Burns](/wiki/Ken_Burns "Ken Burns")'s 1981 documentary *[Brooklyn Bridge](/wiki/Brooklyn_Bridge_(film) "Brooklyn Bridge (film)")*.[[420]](#cite_note-425) It is also described in *[Seven Wonders of the Industrial World](/wiki/Seven_Wonders_of_the_Industrial_World "Seven Wonders of the Industrial World")*, a [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") docudrama series with an accompanying book,[[421]](#cite_note-426) as well as *Chief Engineer: Washington Roebling, The Man Who Built the Brooklyn Bridge*, a biography published in 2017.[[422]](#cite_note-427) The Brooklyn Bridge and Emily Roebling's role in its construction were featured in season 2, episode 5 of the TV series *[The Gilded Age](/wiki/The_Gilded_Age_(TV_series) "The Gilded Age (TV series)")*.[[423]](#cite_note-428) In early 2026, the [Metropolitan Museum of Art](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art") featured presentation drawings from the construction of the bridge in its exhibit *The Brooklyn Bridge Up Close*.[[424]](#cite_note-429)
